Context:
Strong banca fees with favorable NPL support earnings ACB released inline results for FY 2019 with NPAT-MI of VND6.0tn (USD257mn, +16.7% YoY), completing 99% of our 2019 full-year forecast. FY 2019 ROA and ROE stood at 1.7% and 24.6%, respectively. All QoQ figures below are comparisons between Q4 2019 and Q3 2019. Solid 16.9% YoY growth (+10.1% QoQ) in net interest income while NIM remained stable at 3.55%. ACB’s FY 2019 credit growth reached 16.6%. Although interest earning asset (IEA) yields tracked up by seven bps YoY (+7 bps QoQ) to 8.29% in FY 2019, the cost of funds jumped by 15 bps YoY (-12 bps QoQ) to 5.01%. We suspect that a 1.5x increase in valuable papers balance from USD356mn to USD894mn in the last 12 months resulted in the above increase in cost of funds in FY 2019 — despite a 161-bp boost in ACB’s CASA ratio to 19.1%. Net fee income (including FX) soared while recovery income experienced negative YoY growth. Net fee income (including FX) grew at 33.8% YoY, which was driven by a 151% jump in bancassurance fees that contributed 48% to NoII. In contrast, we observed an overall YoY slowdown in the magnitude of recovery in 2019, which led to a 19.1% decrease in FY 2019 of recovery income to VND1.4tn (USD62mn) and contributed 38% of NoII. In total, NoII contributed 24.8% to total operating income in FY 2019, which was a 140-bp decline YoY. Low provisioning expenses in FY 2019 made up for a shortfall in NoII. ACB reported provision expenses of VND274bn (USD12mn) vs VND932bn (USD40mn) in FY 2018. We suspect the bank engaged in a reversal of the specific provision account in a vicinity of VND400bn (USD17mn) during the year; however, the presentation of the provision balance in its financial statement makes it difficult to work out precisely. Strong asset quality as evidenced by the lowest NPL ratio in the sector, accompanied by negligible write-offs in FY 2019. NPL ratio dropped by 18 bps YoY to 0.54%. Loan loss reserve (LLR) soared to 175.5% of NPLs in 2019 vs 151.9% in 2018. Write-offs/gross loans of 0.09% compared with 0.1% that was observed in 2018.

Context:
tôi). Lợi nhuận ròng sau thuế (LNST) trừ lợi ích CĐTS đạt 68,7 tỷ đồng (3,1 triệu USD), tăng 4% so với năm trước, hoàn thành 12% dự báo của chúng tôi. Cần lưu ý rằng quý 1 thường là mùa thấp điểm cho GMD do hoạt động kinh doanh chủ yếu được dẫn dắt từ hoạt động xuất nhập khẩu và sản xuất, thường khá thấp vào giai đoạn đầu năm. Trong quý 1, mảng kinh doanh logistics đã thay thế mảng cảng để trở thành động cơ tăng trưởng doanh thu chính. Mảng logistics ghi nhận mức tăng trưởng 8% trong doanh thu do tiếp tục tăng trưởng hệ thống nhà kho với một loạt các nhà kho mới (DC3, và trung tâm phân phối DC) đi vào hoạt động vào cuối năm 2015. Trong khi đó, mảng cảng có mức tăng trưởng doanh thu đi ngang do tăng trưởng chững lại của lượng container xử lý tại cảng Hải Phòng trong quý 1. Mảng cảng đi ngang so với quý 1/2015 với 418 tỷ đồng doanh thu. Lượng hàng hóa xử lý tại cảng Nam Hải - Đình Vũ và Nam Hải đạt lần lượt 117.000 TEUs (+5% YoY) và 55.000 TEUs (-10% YoY), tổng số lượng hàng hóa xử lý tại cả hai cảng đi ngang so với cùng kỳ năm ngoái. Cảng Nam Hải chứng kiến mức hàng hóa xử lý giảm 10% do Tết Nguyên đán kéo dài sang tháng 2, thường là tháng cao điểm cho hoạt động cảng trong quý 1, lượng hàng hóa xử lý tháng 2 thường chiếm 40-45% tổng khối lượng quý 1/2015. ở cảng Nam Hải - Đình Vũ, mức tăng trưởng hàng hóa xử lý 5% là khá ấn tượng trong bối cảnh mức tăng trưởng ở mức thấp trong hầu hết các cảng trong khu vực Hải Phòng trong quý 1/2016; diễn biến mạnh mẽ này tiếp tục là bằng chứng cho lượng khách hàng ổn định của cảng và vị trí thuận lợi ở hạ nguồn gần cửa sông của cảng này. Nhìn chung, trong quý 1, càng Nam Hải - Đình Vũ vận hành với hiệu suất hoạt động 94% (chưa tính đến công suất tăng thêm tư việc hoàn thành depot Nam Hải), trong khi đó cảng Nam Hải vận hành khoảng 120% so với công suất thiết kế. Vào đầu tháng 4/2016, 3ha đầu tiên của depot mới Nam Hải đã hoàn thành nhưng hiện chỉ đang phục vụ các container rỗng. Dự kiến Depot mới sẽ có thể vận hành tất cả mọi hoạt động khi được chính quyền thành phố Hải Phòng cấp phép. Theo GMD, thủ tục pháp lý và quy định của kho chứa này dự kiến hoàn thành trong quý 2/2016, thời điểm sẽ hoàn thành thêm 4ha depot mới, nâng tổng diện tích của depot mới lên 7ha, và giúp nâng tổng công suất xử lý của cảng Nam Hải Đình Vũ lên đạt mức công suất đạt 650.000 – 700.000 TEUs. (tăng 40% so với công suất thiết kế). Doanh thu logistics tăng 8,3% so với năm trước đạt 431 tỷ đồng (19,3 triệu USD). Dịch vụ logistics cốt lõi có hoạt động kinh doanh tốt hơn mảng tàu biển do những nguyên nhân sau: Mảng kinh doanh tàu vận chuyển (chiếm 40-50% doanh thu logistics). GMD đã thanh lý 1 tàu tiếp vận (feeder) trong quý 1 nhằm nỗ lực cải thiện năng lực sinh lời của đội tàu. GMD sở hữu 4 tàu feeder, trong đó 3 tàu được vận hành bởi các

Context:
Tiên phong trong mảng bao bì nhựa bền vững CTCP Nhựa An Phát Xanh (AAA) là nhà sản xuất bao bì nhựa hàng đầu Việt Nam, chủ yếu cho thị trường xuất khẩu. Không chỉ đạt tăng trưởng kép hàng năm ấn tượng 47% về doanh thu và 27% về LNST sau lợi ích CĐTS trong giai đoạn 2013-2018, công ty còn khiến chúng tôi chú ý vì đi tiên phong trong việc sản xuất các sản phẩm nhựa phân hủy sinh học, hiện đang là một xu hướng tất yếu trên toàn cầu. Chúng tôi dự báo doanh thu từ bao bì nhựa đạt tăng trưởng mạnh cùng với đóng góp từ các mảng kinh doanh mới như phân phối nguyên liệu nhựa và KCN sẽ thúc đẩy tăng trưởng lợi nhuận. AAA đã đầu tư vào các sản phẩm phân hủy sinh học, mà chúng tôi cho rằng không chỉ thể hiện quyết tâm của AAA trong việc sản xuất các sản phẩm thân thiện hơn với môi trường mà còn mang tính quyết định đến triển vọng của công ty khi nhựa sinh học sẽ thay thế trực tiếp cho các sản phẩm nhựa truyền thống của công ty trong dài hạn. Chúng tôi cho rằng dòng tiền hoạt động không cao, đòn bẩy nợ lớn và xu hướng sử dụng các công cụ huy động vốn có tính chất pha loãng như trái phiếu kèm chứng quyền để mở rộng công suất là các vấn đề chính cần được xem xét khi nhà đầu tư quyết định đầu tư vào AAA. Định giá của AAA tỏ ra hấp dẫn tại mức P/E 2019 là 6,9 lần trên cơ sở mục tiêu lợi nhuận công ty đề ra và số cổ phiếu pha loãng (so với P/E trượt 12 tháng của các công ty cùng ngành trong nước là 7,9 lần và trong khu vực là 8,5 lần) vì AAA có các chỉ số cơ bản tốt hơn và triển vọng dài hạn khả quan từ các sản phẩm nhựa phân hủy sinh học. Rủi ro: Giá nhựa đầu vào biến động thất thường, xu hướng giảm sử dụng nhựa chỉ dùng một lần. Doanh thu bao bì nhựa cao, ngoài ra còn có đóng góp của các mảng hoạt động mới. LNST sau lợi ích CĐTS của AAA giai đoạn 2013-2018 đạt tăng trưởng kép hàng năm 27% nhờ mảng bao bì nhựa truyền thống và các lĩnh vực hoạt động mới. Mảng bao bì nhựa của công ty đạt tăng trưởng kép hàng năm về sản lượng 26% trong giai đoạn 2013-2018 nhờ mở rộng công suất cũng như nỗ lực thâm nhập một số thị trường xuất khẩu lớn như EU và Nhật Bản. Lĩnh vực này trước năm 2017 chiếm 80%-90% tổng doanh thu của công ty, và phần còn lại thuộc về mảng phân phối vật liệu nhựa. Vào cuối năm 2017 và năm 2018, AAA đã mở rộng hoạt động phân phối và thâm nhập vào mảng KCN với việc mua lại KCN Việt Hòa – Kenmark. Năm 2019, chúng tôi đánh giá AAA sẽ đạt mục tiêu lợi nhuận 510 tỷ đồng, cao hơn đáng kể so với mức thấp 180 tỷ đồng năm 2018 nhờ giá vật liệu đầu vào thấp và có thêm đóng góp của các mảng kinh doanh mới từ năm 2019 trở đi. Đã đi vào sản xuất các sản phẩm nhựa phân hủy sinh học nhưng việc chuyển đổi cần thời gian. AAA đã bắt đầu đầu tư vào nhựa phân hủy sinh học

Context:
Kỳ vọng lợi nhuận tăng trưởng mạnh sau khi tái cơ cấu Chúng tôi đã đến thăm Tổng Công ty IDICO (HNX: IDC) và một trong những dự án khu công nghiệp (KCN) trọng điểm của công ty tại tỉnh Long An - KCN Hựu Thạnh - đang có tiến độ phát triển tích cực và là yếu tố dẫn dắt tăng trưởng trong trung hạn của IDC. IDC là một trong những chủ đầu tư KCN hàng đầu tại Việt Nam và đã chuyển đổi thành công từ công ty Nhà nước sang công ty tư nhân. Ngoài mảng KCN, IDC còn đầu tư vào các nhà máy thủy điện, kinh doanh điện, xây dựng, thu phí đường bộ và các dự án bất động sản dân dụng/thương mại. Công ty đang tái cấu trúc sau khi trở thành một công ty hoàn toàn thuộc sở hữu của tư nhân, tập trung vào mảng kinh doanh cốt lõi là phát triển KCN và các lĩnh vực liên quan. IDC có kế hoạch mở rộng KCN tại các tỉnh công nghiệp của miền Bắc và miền Nam. Chúng tôi ước tính rằng diện tích KCN cho thuê của IDC sẽ tăng thêm ~1.000 ha – tương ứng hơn 100% - trong giai đoạn 2022-2030. Trong năm 2021, doanh thu của IDC giảm 19% YoY còn 4,3 nghìn tỷ đồng trong khi LNST sau lợi ích CĐTS giảm 48% YoY còn 453 tỷ đồng. Lợi nhuận giảm chủ yếu do ghi nhận hồi tố đối với cho thuê đất KCN tại KCN Mỹ Xuân A trong năm 2020. IDC đang giao dịch với P/B điều chỉnh ước tính là 3,1 lần, nhìn chung phù hợp với trung vị P/B điều chỉnh của các công ty cùng ngành là 3,2 lần. Tuy nhiên, chúng tôi cho rằng IDC xứng đáng có mức định giá cao hơn so với mức trung bình của các công ty cùng ngành do có diện tích đất còn có thể cho thuê lớn tại các quỹ đất hiện hữu và các dự án lớn trong kế hoạch. Rủi ro: Tiến độ phê duyệt KCN trong tương lai bị trì hoãn; tiến độ phát triển các dự án KCN trong tương lai chậm hơn dự kiến; quá trình tái cơ cấu kéo dài. Ghi nhận doanh số bán đất KCN tăng tốc, thúc đẩy tăng trưởng lợi nhuận. IDC chuyển đổi phương thức ghi nhận doanh số cho thuê đất KCN, theo đó, số dư doanh thu chưa thực hiện đối với các KCN đã lấp đầy sẽ được ghi nhận toàn bộ vào doanh thu trong năm hoàn thiện dự án. Chúng tôi tin rằng phương thức này sẽ thúc đẩy tăng trưởng lợi nhuận của IDC trong tương lai, đồng thời phù hợp với dòng tiền từ việc bán đất KCN và giúp công ty linh hoạt hơn trong việc phân bổ lợi nhuận giữ lại để chia cổ tức và/hoặc hỗ trợ các khoản đầu tư mới. Tính đến cuối năm 2021, tổng số dư doanh thu chưa thực hiện của IDC là 6,3 nghìn tỷ đồng - khoảng 165% vốn chủ sở hữu. Ngoài ra, chúng tôi kỳ vọng rằng IDC sẽ ưu tiên phương thức ghi nhận một lần đối với các giao dịch cho thuê đất KCN trong tương lai. Mở rộng kế hoạch phát triển KCN mạnh mẽ sẽ đảm bảo triển vọng trung và dài hạn. Tính đến cuối năm 2021, IDC còn tổng cộng 868 ha diện tích đất có thể cho thuê. Trong giai đoạn 2022-2030, IDC có

HPG 2013 targets VCSC forecast Valuation Strong 1Q result – good year ahead In 1Q13, the company achieved revenue of VND3,934bn (-3% yoy), and net profit of VND480bn (+140% yoy). The strong result is due to i) the improvement in gross margin, thanks to the fall in input cost, ii) the fall in interest expense, thanks to the fall in the interest rate, iii) and the one-off financial gain (VND105bn) from the disposal of a subsidiary. On the international market, the scrap price has fallen by 15% ytd, and the billet has fallen by 8% ytd. The fall in the material price has increased the company’s gross margin from 13.4% in 1Q12 to 17.6% in 1Q13. Domestic lending rates have been falling thanks to aggressive policy rate cuts by the central bank. By our calculations, HPG’s annualised quarterly interest rate has fallen from 12% in 1Q12 to 5% in 1Q13 – in absolute terms, the company’s interest expense fell from VND176bn in 1Q12 to VND81bn in 1Q13. In 1Q, the company sold their stake in Yen Phu Investment and Mineral for an estimated pretax profit of VND105bn. Given the 1Q result, the company achieved 21% their full year revenue target, and 40% their full year net profit target. We note that revenue and net profit from real estate has not been booked in 1Q yet. Figure 2: HPG Targets for 2013 Source: HPG, VCSC For the FY13, the company targets to achieve revenue of VND18,500bn (+10% yoy), and net profit of VND1,200bn (+16% yoy). Of this net profit, the real estate contributes VND100bn. In FY12, the net profit excluding real estate and the financial provision (VND164bn) was VND1,077bn. For FY13 net profit target excluding real-estate is VND1,100bn (+2%). Upgrade FY13 outlook Figure 3: VCSC earnings forecast Source: VCSC forecast We increase our revenue forecast from VND17,502bn to VND18,318bn as we book 20% revenue from the Mandarin Garden project (we did not book revenue from Mandarin in our previous forecast) and increase our steel sale volume forecast. We increase our gross margin profit from VND2,240bn to VND3,020bn to account for the gross profit from the Mandarin Garden, and the better than expected margin from the steel division. We lower our interest expense from VND719bn to VND440bn as interest rates have fallen sharply. As a result, we increase our net profit forecast from VND897bn to VND1,574bn.

Context:
Kế hoạch 2018 đặt thấp khiến cổ đông không hài lòng Tổng Công ty Phân bón và Hóa chất Dầu khí (DPM) đã tổ chức ĐHCĐ hôm nay. Cổ đông đã đặt ra nhiều câu hỏi về mục tiêu 2018, việc điều chỉnh thuế GTGT và tình hình thoái vốn của Chính phủ. Mục tiêu năm 2018 quá thấp, theo đó doanh thu dự kiến tăng 7,3% và LNST giảm 47,6%, theo ban lãnh đạo do chi phí khấu hao nhà máy NH3/NPK cao. Mục tiêu này thấp hơn so với kỳ vọng của chúng tôi và giải thích của ban lãnh đạo không thỏa mãn được cổ đông. Đại diện của Tập đoàn Dầu khí quốc gia Việt Nam (PVN) cho biết tập đoàn sẽ đẩy nhanh tốc độ thoái vốn trong năm 2018 và kế hoạch giảm cổ phần xuống 36% hiện đang được xem xét. Để chuẩn bị cho kế hoạch này, DPM đề xuất tăng mức trần sở hữu cho khối ngoại từ 49% lên 100% và đã được chấp thuận. Chúng tôi cho rằng sẽ điều chỉnh giảm dự báo một chút, chủ yếu vì KQLN Quý 1 vừa công bố kém còn mục tiêu cả năm lại thấp. Kế hoạch kinh doanh thấp năm 2018, khiến cổ đông không hài lòng. DPM đặt kế hoạch doanh thu 2018 đạt 8,6 nghìn tỷ đồng (+7,3% YoY) và LNST đạt 371 tỷ đồng (-47,6% YoY). Ban lãnh đạo lý giải kế hoạch thấp này đến tư chi phí khấu hao cao cho nhà máy NH3/NPK khoảng 500 tỷ đồng. Dù vậy, cổ đông vẫn không hài lòng với câu trả lời này và bay tỏ lo ngại về việc quản lý chi phí Bán hàng, Hành chính và Quản lý (SG&A) của công ty. Chúng tôi đồng tình với quan điểm này khi LNST 2018 chỉ hoàn thành 54,8% dự báo cả năm của chúng tôi. Nhà máy NH3/NPK chuẩn bị đi vào hoạt động trong quý 2/2018. Nhà máy NH3 đã kết thúc giai đoạn chạy thử và bắt đầu hoạt động thương mại trong tháng 4/2018, trong khi nhà máy NPK chỉ vừa hoàn thành chạy thử, và công ty kỳ vọng sẽ sản xuất được khoảng 55.000 tấn NPK trong quý 2/2018, tương ứng với dự báo của chúng tôi. Chúng tôi nhiều khả năng sẽ giữ quan điểm rằng sự đóng góp đầy đủ của các nhà máy này sẽ tạo ra thêm 10-15% lợi nhuận vào năm 2020. Thay đổi thuế GTGT có khả năng đi có hiệu lực vào giữa năm 2018. Đề xuất điều chỉnh thuế GTGT, vốn sẽ phân loại phân bón vào diện được miễn trừ thuế GTGT đầu vào, có thể là yếu tố hỗ trợ giá cho DPM năm 2018. Chủ tịch HĐQT cũng chia sẻ là Quốc hội đã đồng ý nguyên tắc, nhưng vẫn cần đợi quyết định chính thức trong kỳ họp Quốc hội chính thức trong tháng 6/2018. Khi quyết định này chính thức có hiệu lực trong năm 2019, sẽ giúp giảm 20-30% chi phí và nâng giá mục tiêu của chúng tôi thêm 10-15%. Thông qua cổ tức tiền mặt 2.000 đồng/CP năm 2017 (lợi suất 10,5%). DPM đã thanh toán tạm ứng cổ tức tiền mặt 1.000 đồng/CP vào cuối năm 2017, và sẽ thanh toán phần còn lại sau ĐHCĐ này. As such, we studied Alfamart’s story to understand what can be expected of BHX going forward and what potential lies ahead for this new chain. We do this with the understanding that MWG will try a variety of formats with its first 15-20 stores before choosing the most suitable one to scale up. Context:
Wood and quartz businesses to elevate core earnings We maintain our BUY rating on PTB, which is premised on its extensive stone mine portfolio, positive prospects of its new quartz business and long-term growth of Vietnam's wooden furniture export industry as well as domestic car consumption. We raise our target price (TP) by 17% as we roll it over to YE2021, factor in a 1.2-ppt decrease in our house cost of equity to 13.0% and lift our aggregate 2020F-2023F NPAT-MI by 2% thanks to wood revenue, which is partly offset by projected lower capacity utilization for the quartz business. We forecast core NPAT-MI (excluding real estate) will post a 2020F-2023F CAGR of 19% thanks to softer COVID-19 disruptions, a rising quartz contribution and wood export growth. We also expect PTB's condo project to complete 75%/25% of its handover in 2021/2022 (vs 100% in 2021 in our previous report) and contribute 26%/8% to 2021F/2022F NPAT-MI. Our TP puts PTB's core 2021F PER at 8.8x vs the 5Y average peer TTM PER of 11.6x. In our view, this discount is warranted due to PTB’s conglomerate structure and weak free cash flow. Potential risks to our positive view: sluggish domestic construction activity and car consumption due to adverse macro developments; new tariffs on Vietnamese wooden furniture; sluggish new business execution (real estate and quartz). Wood remains robust thanks to elevated online furniture demand in the US and favorable trade flows. In 9M 2020, wood gross profit (GP) soared 50% YoY as wooden furniture exports (84% of wood sales) jumped 67% YoY, which was driven by strong online shopping demand in the US amid social distancing restrictions. We forecast wood sales/GP will record CAGRs of 15%/15% during 2020F-2023F, which is underpinned by PTB’s proven track record, Vietnam’s competitive labor cost and supply-chain diversification away from China. We forecast a 17% CAGR during 2020F-2023F for stone GP after a 21% slump in 2020F due to COVID-19. In 9M 2020, stone GP dipped 18% YoY amid a 1% slide in domestic stone sales and a 9.6-ppt YoY GPM compression, which was due to PTB’s price discounts to offload its inventory. Looking forward, we forecast the natural stone segment’s GP will post a 2020F-2023F CAGR of 7% off a low base. Meanwhile, we expect PTB's new quartz factory, which started operation in October (later than our previous expectation of July), will operate at 24%/35% capacity in Q4 2020/2021 vs 30%/60% in our previous report. Accordingly, quartz’s contribution to stone GP will rise from 2% in 2020F to 19% in 2023F. According to PTB, the US and Australia are its main quartz markets. Domestic auto sales recovery in recent months bodes well for PTB’s auto segment post-2020. We project auto sales/GP will slump 40%/58% in 2020 due to sluggish demand. That said, we expect this segment will return to double-digit growth rates from 2021 — in line with the industry. 9M 2020: Earnings slump as COVID-19 hurt stone, auto segments Figure 1: PTB’s 9M 2020 results Source: PTB, VCSC Real estate project update: Construction progress is on track; however, we tone down our handover assumptions due to slower-than-expected sales In late October 2020, PBT held a topping out and sales event for its Phu Tai Residence condo project. Per management, the project’s construction progress is on track for handover by YE2021. Nevertheless, the project’s sales progress is trailing our expectation with pre-sales having reached ~40% of total units up to now. Due to this sales progress, we now assume this project will recognize 75% of its revenue in 2021 and 25% in 2022 instead of 100% in 2021

Context:
LG plans to expand its facilities in KBC’s upcoming Trang Due 3 IP in Hai Phong, which is close to LG’s existing factories in the Trang Due 2 IP. Upcoming favorable regulations for seaports and IP developers Favorable regulations to increase bracket charges for container handling services. Some port service charges for seaport operators in Vietnam are regulated by the MoT, including major charges such as container handling (loading/unloading) fees. The MoT regulates these fees by setting bracket charges while seaport operators are required to charge their clients between the floor and the cap. The MoT is proposing a draft circular to replace prevailing Circular 54/2018/TT-BGTVT. According to the initial draft circular, the plans are for the floor charges for container handling fees to be increased by around 10% annually from 2021-2023 for the Hai Phong port cluster. Moreover, bracket charges (floor and cap charges) for container handling fees will be increased by around 10% in 2021 and 10% in 2023 for both the Cai Mep port cluster and Lach Huyen deep-sea ports. Nevertheless, the Government has not approved this draft circular. Per GMD, the draft circular will be approved in Q2 2022 and the timeline for the increases will be moved forward compared the initial plan. New regulations on IP and economic zone (EZ) management to improve regulatory consistency and help to attract FDI. The MoPI released a draft decree on IP and EZ management in June 2021, which will replace prevailing Decree no.82/2018/ND-CP. The publication timeline for the new official decree has not yet been announced. Based on our understanding, key changes in the draft decree compared to prevailing regulations include (1) provincial governments having more authority to approve IP/EZ projects, (2) new regulations on IP development financed by advance payments from tenants, (3) supplementing solutions for a surge in IP land leasing prices, (4) more comprehensive regulations and guidance on IP models, and (5) other more comprehensive regulations on export processing companies and economic zones. Overall, we believe the new regulations from the draft decree will allow local government authorities and IP developers to resolve existing obstacles, in turn accelerating the approval processes for new IP and IP expansion projects. Additionally, the draft decree provides clearer and more comprehensive guidance and regulations for IP models (including auxiliary, specialized, ecological IPs and the IP - urban - service model). Recommendations for 2022 For a play on the aviation, logistics and infrastructure sectors, we pick air cargo terminal operator SCS. In addition, we believe VJC can be a target for investors who have a long investment horizon and want to have exposure to the rebound of the airline sector over the next five years. SCS has a strong market and financial position in the air cargo terminal sub-sector, which we expect to benefit from currently favorable conditions of value chains amid high maritime container shipping costs and manufacturers’ prioritization of time. During the early recovery of the airline industry, we prefer low-cost carrier VJC among Vietnam’s two listed carriers due to (1) its relatively more resilient business performance — even if the COVID-19 situation worsens — from its effective cost control that is driven by its superior operation cost management and young, fuel-efficient fleet; (2) demand from leisure travelers — who prefer lower airfares — being expected to recover faster than demand from business travelers; and (3) growth potential as COVID-19 subsides from the airline’s expansion in short to medium-haul international routes. Regarding the industrial real estate sector, we pick LHG — a leading IP developer in Long An Province — and PHR — a rubber company strategically shifting

Context:
Retail sales growth accelerates in Q3 2019 PNJ delivered upbeat NPAT growth of 17% YoY in Q3 2019, mainly driven by a recovery in the high-margin retail (jewelry and watch) segment in Q3 2019. Recall that PNJ’s retail sales fell YoY in Q2 2019 due to out-of-stock problems after it implemented a new ERP system in late March 2019. While the recovery in the retail segment played out in Q3 2019 as we expected, its magnitude slightly trailed our expectation. Therefore, we see potential small downside risk to our full-year earnings projection, pending a fuller review. YoY retail sales growth bounced back firmly in Q3 2019. We estimate that PNJ’s total retail sales advanced ~14% YoY in Q3 2019 (+6% YoY in July, +14% YoY in August and +23% YoY in September 2019) vs a 4% YoY decline in Q2 2019. In our view, this rebound is primarily attributed to the fact that, starting in August 2019, PNJ has fully recovered from the aforementioned out-of-stock problems. Per PNJ, in August 2019, its factory operated at 100% of the run-rate capacity prior to the ERP roll-out vs only 50% in April 2019. Overall, retail sales rose ~12% YoY in 9M 2019, per our estimate, on the back of single-digit same-store-sales growth (SSSG), 21 new gold stores and full contributions from stores opened in 2018. Retail sales growth was stronger outside of HCMC. Per PNJ, retail sales outside of HCMC jumped ~20% YoY in 9M 2019. Meanwhile, HCMC stores, which accounted for roughly half of PNJ’s retail sales, only saw low-single digit sales growth in 9M 2019. Store expansion decelerated in Q3 2019 as the pace of securing locations trailed management’s plan. PNJ opened only five new gold stores in Q3 2019 compared to 16 new gold stores in H1 2019. Per PNJ, the deceleration in store openings was because (1) there was a broad-based slowdown in the roll-out of third-party commercial malls that hindered the expansion of PNJ’s kiosk-in-mall format and (2) market competition for locations intensified, which led to increasing rents as well as difficulties for PNJ to secure satisfactory locations. As of end-Q3 2019, PNJ’s jewelry store count reached 345 stores, consisting of 278 gold stores, 63 standalone silver stores and four CAO Fine Jewelry stores. We will further discuss with management regarding the outlook of store openings in Q4 2019 and beyond. Retail GPM continued to widen YoY in Q3 2019 on the back of a ballooning contribution from gemstone jewelry. PNJ continues to see increased consumer preference for premium products, particularly gemstone jewelry. As a result, retail GPM broadened by 2.9 ppts YoY to reach 33.7% in 9M 2019 (including a 1.2-ppt YoY expansion to 31.6% in Q3 2019). Selling expenses ramped up, suggesting that PNJ resumed marketing activities in Q3 2019. Selling expenses soared 27% YoY in Q3 2019 as PNJ has resumed its promotion campaigns since July 2019 after it put them on hold during Q2 2019 due to the aforementioned out-of-stock issues. This should stage for a further acceleration in retail sales growth in Q4 2019, in our view. Roll-out of watch business trails our expectation. We did not observe any breakthroughs in PNJ’s watch retailing segment in terms of store expansion, sales per store or new product launches in Q3 2019. PNJ’s Q3 2019 and 9M 2019 results Source: PNJ

Weaker Q1 2023 than expected; order backlog to July GDT released Q1 2023 results with revenue slumping 42% YoY and NPAT nosediving 63% YoY, completing 12% and 8% of our forecasts, respectively. We see potential downside risks to our forecasts, pending a fuller review. Weak consumer demand and destocking across the value chain dampened customer orders. However, the situation eased as Q1 2023 revenue was flat QoQ despite Q1 being a low season. GDT’s order backlog fills the company’s production capacity to July 2023, which is behind its normal schedule but in line with other wooden product exporters in our coverage. The order backlogs of the furniture and small-product businesses of GDT are progressing similarly. GDT’s GPM compressed to 27% in Q1 2023 vs 32% in Q1 2022 and 30% in Q4 2022. However, the acquisition of the wooden furniture factory has not been finalized due to legal issues with land-use rights. As such, Q1 2023 results do not include this factory. GDT’s workforce shrank to 918 as of end-Q1 2023 vs 1,075 as of YE2022 and 1,223 as of YE2021. We expect earnings to recover going forward thanks to restocking and the contribution of the new furniture factory. GDT’s Q1 2023 results Source: GDT, VCSC

Context:
Conservative 2023 guidance amid positive 2M 2023 results We attended QNS’s 2023 AGM in Quang Ngai Province on April 1. The meeting focused on the business outlook for 2023 and 2M 2023 performance, which showed better-than-expected sugar sales. We see potential upside to our current forecasts, pending a fuller review. 2023 business guidance includes revenue of VND8.4tn (USD343mn; +1% YoY) and NPAT of VND1.0tn (USD41mn; -16% YoY). We note that QNS has a history of surpassing its profit guidance. Specifically, QNS exceeded its NPAT-MI guidance by 37% in 2021 and 28% in 2022. Shareholders approved the last cash dividend for FY2022 of VND1,500/share (yield of 3.7%); the record and payment dates for this dividend are April 18 and April 27, respectively. Including this amount, the FY2022 cash dividend will total VND3,000/share (yield of 7.5%). FY2023 dividend (either cash or stock): At least 15% on par value. ESOP scheme for FY2023: QNS will issue (1) 1% of its outstanding shares if total YoY growth in net profit before depreciation ranges from 7.5% to 15%, (2) 2% of its outstanding shares if YoY growth in total net profit before depreciation ranges from 15% to 20%, or (3) 3% of its outstanding shares if total net profit before depreciation grows by more than 20% YoY. These shares would be issued at a price equivalent to QNS’s book value as of YE2023 and have a three-year lock-up period. Sugar revenue soared 142% YoY in 2M 2023 due to elevated sugar prices and volume. QNS plans to produce 160,000 tonnes (+46% YoY) and 20,000 tonnes (flat YoY) of refined standard (RS) and refined extra (RE) sugar in 2023, respectively. In addition, management expects sugar prices will continue to be elevated in 2023 thanks to robust demand backed by the Government’s anticircumvention tariffs on sugar imports from ASEAN countries. In 2M 2023, sugar volume increased 140% YoY and revenue increased 142% YoY, per management. QNS is also considering investing VND500bn (USD21mn) in a new sugar warehouse system in 2023-2024. Soy milk revenue increased 6% YoY in 2M 2023 mainly due to selling price hikes. The revenue growth in 2M 2023 was driven by a 5% YoY selling price hike and 1% YoY increase in volume, indicating weak consumption among consumers due to the economic slowdown. According to management, five new SKUs launched in 2018-2022 contributed 10%-15% to the company’s soy milk revenue in 2022. Going forward, management expects to launch a new premium soya product in 2023 to acquire new customers and maintain its dominant soy milk market share; additionally, this new product has higher selling prices than existing products, which would lift the average selling price of the soy milk category. QNS also expects to launch new plant-based meat and beverage products over the long term to capture rising demand for nutritious drinks and expand its product portfolio. High input soybean costs weigh on soy milk profit margins, which is partly mitigated by increasing selling prices and the broadening product portfolio. According to management, soybean costs were locked in at relatively high prices for H1 2023 due to lower supplies of soybeans in the global market. However, QNS expects soy milk GPM to remain flat YoY as the company states that the broadening contribution from premium products and selling price hikes will help to mitigate the impacts of elevated input costs on soy milk profit margins.

Context:
d Sizeable bank with a pending turnaround story Corporate loans dominated SHB’s loan book at 50% of loans as of 9M 2020 (SME 30%, retail 20%). SHB’s management targets to pivot toward the retail segment in the next five years. SHB is still in the process of resolving legacy assets (which we define as non-performing assets that were part of the consolidated balance sheet following the merger between SHB and Habubank in 2012 plus SHB’s exposure to Vinashin’s default in 2010). We expect SHB will fully resolve its legacy asset issue by YE2022. SHB’s 9M 2020 PBT of VND2.6tn achieved 80% of FY2020 guidance of VND3.3tn. Management projects 2021F PBT of VND5.8tn — 77% higher than FY2020 PBT guidance. In our view, this 2021 PBT guidance is aggressive given the additional provisioning required for legacy assets. Valuation looks fully priced at 1.3x 2021F P/B against the sector median of 1.4x and STB (another bank with larger than average legacy problem assets) at 1.2x with a consensus 2021F ROE at 9.1% vs the sector median at 17.7% and STB at 8.4% (sector medians based on our coverage). There is potential for a turnaround story in the next two years, in our view. SHB has a similarly sized loan book to the largest listed private banks such as ACB, MBB and TCB, but it has struggled to match the profitability of these peers primarily due to the burden of provisioning for legacy assets (see pages 12-15) and lower NIM. A major component of SHB’s legacy assets relates to loans extended to Vinashin, although history shows that SHB was only one bank among a lineup of blue-chip lenders to Vinashin. We estimate that SHB’s total net legacy assets, including related accrued interest, stood at 2.8% of total assets as of 9M 2020 and we believe this will continue to trend down, assuming a healthy macroeconomic backdrop. NIM expansion relies on decreasing funding cost. SHB’s IEA yield is high with an average level of ~8.2% from 2016-2019, which is at the higher end of the range of private banks under our coverage. However, the average NIM level during the same period was ~2.2% — at least one percentage point below private bank peers as a result of higher funding cost. Unlike STB, which has room to leverage its regulated LDR from its 9M 2020 level of 73% to improve NIM, SHB’s regulated LDR was 80% (9M 2020) vs the cap of 85%. Therefore, we believe the upside on NIM relies on optimizing the funding structure by increasing the CASA ratio from the current low level of 7% (9M 2020). SHB targets to pivot toward the retail segment over the next few years. Corporate loans occupied around half of SHB’s loan book as of 9M 2020. We believe SHB can leverage its physical customer touchpoints as well as its corporate client base to expand its retail business. SHB’s fee income performance is lackluster compared to peers but could improve alongside progress in pivoting toward retail banking via cross-selling, in our view. In addition, SHB signed an exclusive bancassurance contract in 2017 with Dai-ichi Life and the current subpar performance in banca sales is another area the bank can improve to increase NFI. Company Overview History and corporate profile Saigon-Hanoi Commercial Joint Stock Bank (HNX: SHB), was founded on November 13, 1993, with an initial charter capital of only VND400mn. At the time of its establishment, SHB was known as Nhon Ai Bank and was based in Can Tho with agriculture loans being a target segment. SHB defines its establishment phase to encompass the years of

Rising urea export potential for DCM and DPM At the beginning of 2020, COVID-19 negatively impacted the global fertilizer market as the pandemic caused 1) logistical difficulties and supply disruption due to labor immobility in many countries and 2) indirectly lowered fertilizer demand given declining demand for agriculture products (however, the smallest decline in demand was for rice and grains). Due to logistical and transportation difficulties, there has been oversupply in some markets and also supply disruptions in other markets. From April to September 2020, thanks to favorable weather conditions for India’s autumn crop season, there was rising urea demand from this country; meanwhile, China’s urea production dropped due to declining coal input feedstock. In 9M 2020, we estimate DCM’s export volume rose 2.5x YoY with rising exports to Cambodia, India, Thailand and Brazil accounting for most of Vietnam’s total export volume. DCM is the leading urea exporter in Vietnam thanks to its granular urea products that are large and easier to store and handle, thus enable long distance transportation and storage. For the 2021 outlook, we expect a promising export outlook for urea producers in Vietnam — especially for DCM. Many countries such as China, India and others in Asia will continue to focus on agriculture (especially rice and grains) to ensure national food security. According to the International Fertilizer Association (IFA), urea (nitrogen) demand will experience the fastest recovery among other kinds of fertilizers as rice demand should be solid while there is a weaker demand recovery for other agriculture products such as fruit, coffee and cashews. In addition, per World Bank’s forecast, favorable growing conditions in India and Australia will support higher urea demand in these countries. Finally, we foresee a potentially lower urea supply from China — the largest urea producer in the world — due to a gas shortage. Earnings to decline in 2023, but valuation is undemanding We cut our target price for HDG by 14% but maintain our BUY rating. Our lower target price is due to our 30% downward revision in the real estate segment’s valuation as we delay the sales launches of HDG’s real estate projects by six months to one year and have a lower valuation for the Charm Villas project. This implies that the real estate segment contributes only 30% to HDG’s valuation. We lower our 2023-2026F aggregate NPAT by 12% as we extend the handovers of Charm Villas Phase 3 over three years (2023-2025) due to the currently weak confidence of homebuyers. We also delay sales launches/handovers of two mid-end real estate projects in HCMC and two real estate projects in Hanoi as there was no significant improvement in legal procedures (see p. 10). We forecast 2023F NPAT-MI to decline 24% YoY with a lower NPAT contribution from the energy segment (-11% YoY), which outweighs higher profit from Charm Villas (+13% YoY) and higher NPAT from the hotel & office leasing segment. We find HDG’s valuation attractive at a TTM PER of 6.7x — a 30% discount vs our selected power and real estate peer groups; its projected 2023 P/E is also ~20% lower vs PC1 (HDG’s most relevant peer). We forecast an average ROE of 20% and an EPS CAGR of 24% in 2022-2025F. HDG has the most visible wind power expansion plan in our coverage with a 540 MW capacity, specific project names/locations in central Vietnam, and inclusion in revised Power Development Plan VII. Downside risk: Slower-than-expected progress of future real estate projects. Upside risks: Lower-than-expected land cost for Charm Villas; groundbreaking of An Phong giant wind project (300 MW) to facilitate Hado Energy IPO. HDG plans to launch sales of Phase 3 (130 units) of Charm Villas in Q2 2023. The selling price is VND90mn/sqm, which is nearly unchanged from Phase 2 and in line with our expectation. However, we assume HDG will finish sales and handovers in 2023-2025 (instead of previously 2023-2024) due to weak confidence among homebuyers. In addition, we lower our total NPAT forecast for Charm Villas from VND2.5tn to VND2.3tn because HDG estimated additional land use fees of ~VND200bn for this project in Q4 2022 and allocated them to that quarter’s result despite not having finalized/paid the fees with the local authority, which is the main reason for the negative GPM in this quarter. The energy segment’s NPAT will decline 11% YoY in 2023, but the segment will continue to provide strong cashflow. We forecast 2023 hydropower NPAT to decline ~50% YoY due to less favorable rainfall vs 2022’s high base. However, we expect the 7A wind farm’s volume to recover to a normal level (+68% YoY) from 2022’s low base when there were abnormally low wind speeds and curtailment; therefore, this wind farm’s 2023 NPAT should jump and support the energy segment’s NPAT. With strong cashflow from the energy segment, HDG’s net gearing at end-2022 dropped to 80% vs 120% at end-2021. vào năm 2025F, nhờ du lịch quốc tế phục hồi và hiệu quả hoạt động được cải thiện. Dịch COVID-19 gây khó khăn cho hàng điện tử nhưng thúc đẩy doanh số bán hàng bách hóa Chúng tôi đã tham dự cuộc họp NĐT của CTCP Đầu tư Thế giới Di động (MWG) vào ngày 20/08/2021. Ban lãnh đạo đã thảo luận về tình hình kinh doanh và triển vọng của MWG trong bối cảnh tình hình COVID-19 hiện tại ở Việt Nam cũng như kế hoạch của MWG để đối phó với những thách thức. Do tình hình COVID-19 hiện tại phức tạp hơn đáng kể so với chúng tôi dự báo - ảnh hưởng đến hoạt động của các cửa hàng TGDD và DMX - chúng tôi nhận thấy nguy cơ điều chỉnh giảm đối với các dự báo hiện tại của chúng tôi, dù cần thêm đánh giá chi tiết. Ban lãnh đạo duy trì quan điểm thận trọng về nhu cầu của người tiêu dùng đối với điện thoại di động và điện máy trong năm 2021 và 2022. Tuy nhiên, nếu các quy định về giãn cách xã hội hiện tại giảm dần trong quý 4/2021, ban lãnh đạo tin rằng TGDD và DMX vẫn có thể đạt được kế hoạch năm 2021 về doanh thu tổng khoảng 94 nghìn tỷ đồng (+7% YoY). Mặt khác, khi dịch COVID-19 làm gián đoạn các kênh bán lẻ truyền thống (ví dụ: chợ truyền thống và cửa hàng tạp hoá) đang thúc đẩy lưu lượng đến các cửa hàng BHX, ban lãnh đạo dự kiến BHX sẽ đạt được mức hòa vốn EBITDA trong cả năm 2021, tốt hơn so với trước đó là mục tiêu đạt điểm hòa vốn EBITDA vào cuối năm 2021. 1. KQKD tháng 7 và 7 tháng đầu năm 2021 1.1. Tổng doanh thu và TGDĐ/ĐMX Tổng doanh thu tăng 10% YoY đạt 9,5 nghìn tỷ đồng vào tháng 7/2021, trong đó tổng doanh thu của TGDĐ và ĐMX giảm 23% YoY khi dịch COVID-19 bùng phát tại Việt Nam. Theo ban lãnh đạo, gần 2.000 cửa hàng TGDĐ và ĐMX bị ảnh hưởng bởi các hạn chế về giãn cách xã hội (cụ thể, các cửa hàng đang tạm thời đóng cửa hoặc hạn chế về số lượng người bên trong cửa hàng) và các cửa hàng này thường đóng góp hơn 80% vào tổng doanh thu của TGDĐ và ĐMX. Với những quy định chặt chẽ hơn về giãn cách xã hội vào tháng 8/2021, ban lãnh đạo dự kiến TGDĐ và ĐMX sẽ đối mặt với nhiều thách thức hơn. Trong 7 tháng đầu năm 2021, tổng doanh thu tăng 12% YoY, trong đó tổng doanh thu của TGDĐ và ĐMX tăng 3% YoY chủ yếu nhờ vào việc tiếp tục triển khai các cửa hàng DMX siêu nhỏ (120-150 m2/cửa hàng), với số lượng cửa hàng tăng thêm 287 đạt 589 trong 7 tháng đầu năm 2021. DMX siêu nhỏ đóng góp 3,5 nghìn tỷ đồng doanh thu trong 7 tháng đầu năm 2021, tương đương 6% tổng doanh thu của TGDĐ và ĐMX. 1.2. Đáng chú ý, doanh thu của BHX đạt 4,2 nghìn tỷ đồng (+133% YoY) vào tháng 7/2021 với doanh thu/cửa hàng tăng từ hơn 1,4 tỷ đồng vào tháng 6/2021 lên hơn 2,1 tỷ đồng vào tháng 7/2021.

Gemalink is expected to support GMD’s medium-term growth Per management, Gemalink is on track with its launch schedule in Q4 2020 in order to capture double-digit container throughput growth in the Cai Mep area (which is expected to be a regional transshipment hub). There has been solid throughput growth in the Cai Mep area as it achieved a five-year CAGR of 23% over 2014-2019, reaffirming Gemalink’s potential. Compared to other deep sea ports in the Cai Mep area, Gemalink can accommodate larger vessels of up to 200,000 DWT vs the maximum 160,000 DWT vessels in other ports. We use the SoTP approach for GMD given the large contribution from its affiliates as well as the company’s intention to divest its non-core assets. We apply an unchanged valuation discount of 10% on GMD in order to factor in its conglomerate structure as well as its limited information access related to its non-core businesses (real estate and rubber plantations). Our SoTP includes: (1) Port and consolidated logistics segments, which we value using a DCF method as these businesses mostly generate predictable cash flows. (2) Saigon Cargo Service (SCS), which is the main contributor to GMD’s net income from affiliates. We value SCS using a DCF method. For more information on our view and valuation of SCS, please see our April 28, 2020 Update

In term of clients and sales channels, THI satisfied 80% of EVN’s demand as it handled the majority of EVN’s project supply needs; however, it only holds 35% market share in the retail market. Products THI produces two main types of transformers: single-phase and three-phase. Both are medium and low voltage measuring transformers. THI usually produces 12,400 single-phase transformers and 8,400 three-phase transformers per year. The value of three-phase transformers can be four to five times higher than single-phase transformers. According to the company, single-phase transformers are primarily used in rural areas for agricultural production, whereas three-phase transformers are used for commercial purposes such as hotels and restaurants or manufacturing purposes found in factories and industrial zones. Figure 27: THI’s products Source: THI, GEX, VCSC Per management, THI’s production technology is one of its biggest competitive advantages as it leads the market. Particularly, THI received a technology license for producing Amorphous transformer from Japan’s Hitachi Metals in Q3 2014. This product is better than traditional transformers with an 80% reduction in loss of power rate during transmission, reduced CO2 emissions and a longer life span (25-30 years). Financial performance in 2015-2018 THI’s revenue contribution is made up of 60% from EVN projects and 40% from retail. Over the period of 2015-2017, revenue grew significantly from VND2tn (USD86mn) to VND2.5tn (USD107mn), implying CAGR of 12.8% primarily due to a surge in sales volume. THI achieved this from selling the new Amorphous metal transformers with support from Hitachi. Notably, a quantity of this product was funded by Japan’s Ministry of Environment due to an agreement between Vietnam and Japan to reduce carbon emissions. While these transformers reduce CO2 emissions, have long lifespan (25-30 years) and reduce power loss up to 80%, they also meet all necessary requirements of both power corporations and construction projects, which results in strong output volume growth. However, revenue dropped by 12.9% YoY to VND2.2tn (USD96.5mn) in 2018 as there was a slowdown in EVN’s investment in upgrading its electricity transmission and distribution system along with northern transformer manufacturers penetrating the southern market. NPAT increased significantly from VND160bn (USD7mn) in 2015 to VND279bn (USD12mn) in 2018, equivalent to CAGR of 20.4%, mainly due to lower SG&A expenses. Particularly, NPAT grew 55.8% YoY to VND160bn (USD6.9mn) in 2015 because of a drop in general selling expenses by 18% YoY to VND122bn (USD5.3mn). Moreover, NPAT still grew at 52.5% YoY to VND321bn in 2017 (USD14mn) from 211bn (USD9.1mn), mainly due to (1) a VND44.3bn (USD1.9mn) reversal of product warranties and (2) VND27.7bn (USD1.2mn) of interest income gained from bond investments. In 2018, despite an appearance of a VND55bn (USD2.3mn) reversal of provision expenses and a VND19.9bn (USD0.9mn) gain from its associate (TBD), NPAT dropped by 13.1% YoY, mainly due to a significant decline of gross profit at 30.2% YoY. Context:
VND8tn rights issue targeted in H1 2021 We attended HVN’s EGM in Hanoi on December 29, 2020. The airline announced 2020 preliminary results with a consolidated net loss of VND14tn/USD609mn and a loss after adjustments of VND12tn/USD522mn vs a net profit of VND2.54tn/USD110mn in 2019. HVN’s shareholders authorized HVN’s BOD to develop a VND8tn share issuance plan and seek final approval from shareholders in an upcoming shareholder meeting. HVN announced one new BOD member, Mr Le Truong Giang, to replace Mr. Luong Tri Thanh, who is retiring. The Government has approved the SBV to back VND4tn of loans for HVN at a favorable rate. In return, the Government will have the right to purchase additional shares to the value of the notional interest expense saving for HVN. HVN proposed that non-government shareholders will also be eligible to participate in the loan package scheme under the same terms. We see potential downside risk to our current valuation for HVN as we have not included the possible dilution from this proposed loan scheme in our projections. Preliminary loss is lower than 2020 guidance and our forecast mainly due to accounting adjustments. HVN announced 2020 preliminary revenue/net loss of VND42.5tn/VND11.6tn (USD1.85bn/USD504mn), respectively. The revenue/loss equate to 131%/80% of HVN's guidance and 97%/77% of our forecasts for HVN’s revenue and loss in 2020, respectively. According to HVN, the preliminary loss should be VND14tn (broadly in line with our forecast) before adjustments for lower depreciation and maintenance allocations, which could be approved by the government. Share issuance plan approved in principle by general shareholders. However final approval will be sought in an upcoming general shareholder meeting. HVN's EGM approved the total value of a rights issue for all shareholders of VND8tn/USD347.83mn, to be completed in H1 2021. The proceeds from the rights issue will be used for working capital, repaying short and long-term debt and will not be used for capex or other non-core activities. The SCIC will participate in the rights issue on behalf of the government. HVN invites shareholders to participate in loan package. The Government has approved a supporting loan package for HVN in which the SBV will refinance banks at 0% to give three-year-term loans to HVN. The banks can thus lend to HVN at a favorable rate. In return, the Government will receive additional new HVN shares at VND10,000 per share to the value of the notional interest saving for the company (based on the difference between HVN’s lowest current borrowing rate in VND and the subsidized lending rate from the banks). Other non-government shareholders can also participate in the loan package under the same terms.

Context:
investment returns. Higher beta funds can have stocks account for more than 90% of the fund assets. More life insurers — including BVH — are planning to launch unit-linked products to gain market share. Currently, only eight out of 18 life insurers in Vietnam offer unit-linked products. Selling unit-linked products requires a high level of internal capabilities as these products are strictly regulated and require complex operational processes as well as strong capital, in our view. However, in order to catch up with intensifying competition in the life sector, companies must be able to offer unit-linked products. Premium through banks surged in 9M 2021. New business premium received through banks grew by 63.9% YoY to VND13.8tn, accounting for 39.0% of the sector’s new business premium. Meanwhile, total direct premium sold through banks grew by 48.3% YoY to VND24.2tn, accounting for 22.0% of the sector’s total direct premium as of 9M 2021. Manulife surpassed BVH in terms of new business premium received through other distribution channels in 9M 2021. Even though the total number of agents at Manulife ranked fourth in the market (Figure xx), we think that Manulife delivered the highest amount of new business premium (VND4.9tn) through other distribution channels (normally associated with agency distribution) in 9M 2021. Sun Life experienced the strongest increase in new business premium received through bancassurance thanks to its exclusive bancassurance deal with ACB. New business premium received through banks for Sun Life increased 6.7x YoY in 9M 2021 (Figure xx), which we believe was mainly due to its engagement with ACB’s large retail customer base. Bond yields continued to track down in 2021, creating upward pressure on mathematical reserve expenses and downward pressure on earnings. Vietnam regulates a discount rate for its reserve calculation based on (1) 80% of a maximum 80% of the average interest rate of Government bonds with a term of more than 10 years that were issued 24 months before the reserve is set aside and (2) 20% of a maximum 70% of the average interest rate of Government bonds with a term of more than 10 years that were issued six months before the reserve is set aside (only applies for 2021). For 2022 onward, the discount rate for the reserve calculation will be based on 100% of a maximum 80% of the average interest rate of Government bonds with a term of more than 10 years that is issued 24 months before the reserve is set aside. Therefore, the plunge in Government bond yields in 2021 exacerbated the burden of providing for mathematical reserves. However, lower bond yields (i.e., higher prices) also provided life insurers an opportunity to sell bonds to offset the increase in mathematical reserve expenses, in our view. Non-life insurance Total direct premium grew 4.5%YoY in 8M 2021. In our 2021 Strategy Report published at the beginning of the year, we expected that direct premium growth of the non-life sector would grow at ~10% YoY in 2021 on the back of our house 2021 GDP forecast of 6.7% at that point in time. However, tightened social measures during the fourth wave of COVID-19 in Vietnam dampened the non-life business in 2021. As of 8M 2021, total direct premium of the non-life insurance sector reached VND37.8tn (+4.5% YoY), equating to lower YoY growth than the 6.9% YoY growth experienced in 8M 2020. The impact of the economic slowdown on the non-life sector was highlighted by -7.8% YoY growth in motor vehicle insurance in 8M 2021. However, four other products among the top five lines of business still experienced a YoY increase (see Figure 22). Annualized

Context:
Another net loss projected for 2022 We attended HVN’s AGM on June 28. The meeting mainly focused on restructuring plans to sustain the airline’s operation. For FY2022, HVN guides for a consolidated loss of VND11.5tn (USD496mn) — 14% less than the loss in 2021. This loss target is 1.5x more than our current projection; as a result, we see downside risk to our current forecast and valuation for HVN, pending a fuller review. HVN’s shareholders approved a proposal allowing the company to divest from its subsidiaries and associates. HVN’s AGM approved the replacement of a BOD member with Mr. Tomoji Ishii being replaced by Mr. Hiroyuki Kometani — another representative of ANA Holdings. Divestments and fleet restructuring are the key drivers to reduce accumulated losses. HVN plans to sell 32 aircraft in 2022-2025, including 26 A321ceo and six ATR 72 via straight selling and/or sale & lease backs. We note that HVN already made plans to sell aircraft in 2021, but management admits the progress has been slow due to abundant supply of aircraft on the market. Moreover, the company has plans to divest from its subsidiaries and associates — with Pacific Airlines as the most significant planned divestment. Management stated there are potential buyers, but the company is waiting for clearer regulations from the Government to process the deal. Delisting risk is more visible, but it may be resolved by further equity issuance. With the projected loss in 2022, HVN’s shareholder equity would be negative in 2022, thus disqualifying the stock from remaining listed on HOSE. The airline plans to increase its charter capital by private placement and/or rights issuance in 2022-2023. However, the equity raising is a part of the restructuring plan that is awaiting Government approval. Fuel cost is the biggest challenge in 2022; HVN is proposing to lift of the cap on airfare. Although HVN’s volume of the domestic air transportation segment in H1 2022 surpassed the H1 2019 level, HVN mainly attributes its large projected loss in 2022 to significantly higher jet fuel prices combined with the cap on domestic airfares. The airline stated that the cap is based on a jet fuel cost of ~USD80/bbl, which is half of the current price. As a result, HVN has asked the Government to (1) lift the cap on airfare and (2) allow airlines to include a fuel surcharge in airfare. Cutting costs and improving efficiency to maintain liquidity in 2022. HVN stated that it has simplified structure of its group, cut labor costs by shifting to performance-based compensation, and negotiated with suppliers to delay payments. As a result, the cash flow in recent months has reached 80% of the 2019 level. Management is also confident that HVN can sustain its liquidity as there has been no significant outbreak of COVID-19 in Vietnam so far in 2022.

Context:
hỗ trợ NIM. Chúng tôi ước tính chi phí vốn 9 tháng đầu năm 2016 giảm 10 điểm cơ bản so với cùng kỳ năm ngoái, trong khi tỷ lệ tín dụng/huy động (tỷ lệ LDR) tăng 144 điểm cơ bản. Thu nhập ngoài lãi giảm chủ yếu do lỗ từ chứng khoán đầu tư. Theo chúng tôi, đây là kết quả của việc ghi nhận dự phòng trái phiếu do 6 công ty phát hành. Lỗ do chứng khoán đầu tư không gây ngạc nhiên do nằm trong kế hoạch. Chúng tôi ước tính, đến cuối tháng chín, ACB đã ghi nhận khoảng 780 tỷ đồng dự phòng cho 6 công ty. Vì vậy, nếu không tính khoản dự phòng trên, thu nhập ngoài lãi, lợi nhuận từ HĐKD trước dự phòng, và lợi nhuận trước thuế tăng lần lượt 12,3%, 33,4%, và 81,8%. Chi phí dự phòng rủi ro tín dụng giảm nhờ việc cải thiện chất lượng tài sản, việc này được phản ánh thông qua cơ cấu nợ ngắn/trung/dài hạn ổn định, giá trị tuyệt đối lãi phải thu giảm (dù dư nợ tăng), và giá trị trái phiếu VAMC giảm theo báo cáo Quý 2. Chúng tôi xin lưu ý rằng về dự phòng cho trái phiếu VAMC, ACB hiện áp dụng phương pháp chặt chẽ hơn so với quy định trong các quý vừa qua. Triển vọng lợi nhuận: Hoạt động cốt lõi đặt nền móng cho phát triển Tuy hoạt động ngân hàng cố lõi đạt kết quả cao, ACB vẫn chịu áp lực về việc giải quyết các vấn đề trên bảng cân đối kế toán. Vì vậy, chúng tôi không điều chỉnh dự báo. Trường hợp ngân hàng đạt kết quả báo cáo khả quan, chúng tôi cho rằng phần lợi nhuận phụ trội từ hoạt động cốt lõi sẽ đưuợc dùng để ghi nhận thêm dự phòng cho các vấn đề tồn đọng, giảm gánh nặng cho các năm tới. Tình hình các vấn đề tồn đọng còn lại của ACB có cải thiện. Chúng tôi ước tính ACB đã giải quyết khoảng 1.800-1.900 tỷ đồng các khoản phải thu từ nhóm 6 công ty trong 9 tháng đầu năm, còn lại khoảng 300-400 tỷ đồng trong Quý 4, theo kế hoạch đã được thông qua. Điều này khá dễ dàng đối với ngân hàng vì có hoạt động cốt lõi lành mạnh. Vì vậy, chúng tôi dự báo ngân hàng có thể tăng dự phòng năm nay để giảm bớt dự phòng bắt buộc năm 2017 và 2018. Đối với hoạt động ngân hàng cốt lõi, chúng tôi vẫn cho rằng ACB sẽ đạt kết quả tốt trong tương lai gần. Lơi nhuận từ thu nhập lãi và thu nhập từ phí dịch vụ và hoa hồng dự kiến sẽ tăng lần lượt 16,2% và 26,4%. Thu nhập lãi dự kiến sẽ tăng vì tăng trưởng tín dụng lên đến 21% (9 tháng đầu năm 2016: 18,1%) và NIM ổn định tại mức 3,3% (2015:3,1%). Theo trao đổi với ACB, ngân hàng dự kiến lợi nhuận trước thuế của hoạt động cốt lõi sẽ vào khoảng 3.200-3.600 tỷ đồng năm 2016-2017. Chúng tôi ước tính lợi nhuận trước thuế 2016 từ hoạt động cốt lõi sẽ là 2.800 tỷ đồng, nếu không tính các vấn đề tồn đọng. Vì vậy, nhằm đạt mục tiêu 2017, hoạt động cốt lõi của ACB sẽ phải tăng 14%-28%, đây là một mức tăng trưởng có thể đạt được, đặc biệt trong bối cảnh hoạt động ngân hàng cốt lõi tăng trưởng 57,5%

With sales volume and profit growth starting to show positive signs, we believe BMP shares have been oversold to an attractive valuation, causing a buy opportunity to emerge. We forecast 5% NPAT-MI CAGR over 2018-2023F as we still expect strong competition to persist. Any new sales initiatives from BMP and/or stronger-than-expected recovery of the property market will provide more upside to this forecast. BMP is now trading at 2019F/2020F PER of 8.3x/8.0x based on our forecast vs the six-month average of the regional peer median of 10.8x despite BMP’s superior profitability (TTM ROE of 17.5% vs 11.5% peer median) and strong financial position (Net D/E of -23.6% vs -11.8% peer median). Figure 1: VCSC’s steel and plastic pipe manufacturers coverage vs VN-Index Source: Bloomberg, VCSC compilation Figure 2: Key metrics of BMP vs peers Source: Bloomberg, VCSC compilation Overview of Vietnam’s plastics industry Mapping Vietnam’s plastics industry Vietnam’s plastics industry is very fragmented. According to the Vietnam Plastic Association (VPA), there are around 2,000 plastic enterprises in Vietnam, of which 80% are private companies and the remaining 20% are FDI enterprises. About half of these are small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs) with charter capital lower than VND500mn (USD21,700). Nearly 80% of plastic enterprises are located in Ho Chi Minh City (HCMC) and nearby provinces such as Dong Nai, Binh Duong, and Long An. The Government is pushing construction and technical plastics. Vietnam’s plastic products are divided into four main segments: packaging plastics (accounting for 38% of market share by volume), household plastics (29%), construction plastic (18%), and high-tech plastics (15%). According to the Government’s long-term blueprint for developing Vietnam’s plastics industry through 2025, the plastics industry will be restructured by gradually reducing the portion of packaging and household plastics and increasing the portion of construction and high-tech plastics. Figure 3: Vietnam plastic industry market size by segment Source: StoxPlus, VPA, VCSC compilation Packaging plastics: Comprising of nearly 400 enterprises, the plastic packaging sector, especially soft packaging and polyethylene terephthalate (PET) food packaging are supporting industries for the consumer goods sector, including packaged food, canned and bottled beverages. Products of this segment includes (polyethylene) PE films/bags, woven sheets/bags, laminated films/bags, biaxially oriented polypropylene (BOPP) films/bags, plates, (high-density polyethylene) HDPE and PE trays and bottles, among others. Household plastics: This segment includes plastic furniture, cabinets, kitchenware, toys and toys, among other products. Local enterprises mainly focus on the mass market with low profit margins whereas FDI enterprises dominate the high-end product range with higher profit margin. Construction plastics: This segment is comprised of two categories: 1) plastic pipes and 2) plastic profile systems (including doors and windows). The growth of these products is supported by 1) a healthy property market and large pipeline of infrastructure projects; 2) the bulky size of construction plastics making transportation difficult and inefficient and thus limiting competition from imported products; and 3) specific consumer tastes in local brands. Technical plastics: This segment is a supporting industry for other industrial sectors, especially for international manufacturers such as Samsung and LG. The products of this industry encompass plastic fittings used in household appliances, consumer electronics, auto and motorcycle assembly and other products. Context:
Core agri/food businesses display positive progress We reiterate our BUY rating on GTN with TP unchanged. As discussed in our initiation report in July 2016, GTN’s P&L will only start reflecting its agri/food-focused business from 2017 onward as the business restructuring process is still ongoing. However, strong profit growth at Vilico, coupled with new branded tea products ready for official launch in October, are reasons to be optimistic about GTN's ability to restructure these businesses successfully, as they did with Ladofoods. GTN’s NPAT dropped 77% in H1 2016 due to restructuring costs for Vinatea and lower net financial income. In H1 2016, Vinatea shored up spending on building its executive team, sales force and distribution system to prepare for the upcoming branded product launch in October 2016. Meanwhile, in H1 2016, GTN recognized VND11 billion (USD0.5 million) of financial income from non-core divestments (100% of PBT), compared to VND19.8 billion (USD0.9 million) in H1 2015. Vinatea’s revenue grew 26% in H1 2016 vs H1 2015 on higher volume and ASP. GTN’s quality-enhancing initiatives bore fruit as Vinatea secured more export contracts at higher ASP. However, H1 2016 GPM was affected by the offloading of obsolete inventory that was in place pre-acquisition. More export shipments and launch of branded products for the domestic market will further bolster Vinatea in H2 2016. Vinatea signed export contracts worth USD9 million in H1 2016, but only recognized 57% of that in H1 2016. Management aims to secure another USD4.5 million worth of contracts in H2 2016. Meanwhile, Vinatea’s new branded products for the domestic market, which are set to launch in October, should carry high GPM of ~50%, according to management. On a standalone basis, Vilico posted 51% NPAT growth on improved procurement of input materials in the dairy business. Vilico’s EBIT margin expanded by 2.5 ppt in H1 2016 vs H1 2015 while its dairy revenue (73% of VLC’s revenue) rose 11% driven by volume growth. GTN’s management expects to consolidate Vilico in Q4 2016, soon after the company completes its private placement involving 100 million shares, which we expect to take place in October 2016. FY17F NPAT +180% vs FY16F on full-year contribution of Vilico and Vinatea’s branded product sales. We are projecting 180% NPAT growth in FY17 vs FY16’s low base for Vinatea, primarily driven by the full-year contribution of its branded products and further ramp-up in exports. On the other hand, Vilico, which will be consolidated for the whole year vs one month in FY16, is forecast to deliver 16% NPAT growth on the back of 15% volume growth and 5% ASP growth in the dairy business, the latter being backed by Vilico’s upgraded branding and packaging, together with distribution network expansion following GTN’s takeover. Chúng tôi duy trì dự báo LNST sau lợi ích CĐTS năm 2022 ở mức 1,4 nghìn tỷ đồng (+17% YoY) khi chúng tôi tiếp tục kỳ vọng dự án Classia (khởi công vào cuối tháng 9/2021; mở bán sớm khoảng 40 căn vào quý 2/2022) sẽ thúc đẩy lợi nhuận 6 tháng cuối năm 2022. Chúng tôi tiếp tục kỳ vọng tổng doanh số bán hàng giai đoạn 2022-2023 (chủ yếu đến từ các dự án quy mô nhỏ gồm Classia, The Privia và Clarita) sẽ tăng mạnh so với mức cơ sở thấp trong giai đoạn 2020-2021; chúng tôi kỳ vọng công ty sẽ tăng tốc mở bán các dự án này bắt đầu từ 6 tháng cuối năm 2022. Context:
Weaker mobile outlook, pharmacy remains key growth driver We cut our target price (TP) for FRT by 18% and but maintain our OUTPERFORM rating as the stock price has dropped 25% in the last three months. Our lower TP is mainly due to: (1) a 32% cut in our aggregate 2023F-2025F NPAT-MI, primarily owing to our 50% cut in FPT Shop’s aggregate NPAT-MI over the same period, and (2) raising our WACC assumptions due to our higher equity risk premium (ERP) and higher cost of debt, partially offset by rolling over our target price horizon to end-2023. We expect the current global supply shortage of iPhones to put pressure on FRT’s earnings growth as iPhone sales account for 29% of FRT’s overall revenue. Additionally, we expect weak consumption amid economic slowdown to weaken the sales of FPT Shop’s laptops and other smartphone brands. We maintain our view that the Long Chau (LC) pharmacy chain — which is FRT’s growth engine — is on track for a sustainable growth phase with improved profitability. Upside catalysts: Stronger/sooner-than-expected recovery of global iPhones supply. Downside risks: LC’s store rollout and profitability underperforming expectations; stiff competition from MWG; weaker-than-expected discretionary consumption. Weaker consumption outlook coupled with global supply shortage of iPhones to dampen FPT Shop’s growth outlook in 2023. We cut our forecast for FPT Shop’s aggregate 2023F-2025F sales by 22% mainly due to our downward revision in FPT Shop’s SSSG to -8% in 2023 from 15% in our previous forecast as we expect the current global supply shortage of iPhones stemming from China’s commitment to a zero-covid policy will significantly increase the order lead times for iPhones, hence reducing sales volume of the product in Vietnam. This is the main driver of our 50% cut in aggregate forecast NPAT-MI over the same period as FPT Shop has a high operating leverage and narrow net margins. We expect iPhone sales — which accounted for ~35% of FPT Shop’s revenue in 2021 — will continue to drive FRT’s sales in 2024-2025F mainly due to (1) Apple’s more supportive policies in Vietnam and (2) Vietnamese people’s strong appetite for Apple products. However, we believe FPT Shop’s earnings outlook is highly sensitive to China’s COVID restrictions at China Foxconn in the near term due to the high contribution of iPhones to FPT Shop’s total sales. We remain positive on the prospects for LC on sustained sales/store and gross margins. However, we reduce LC’s aggregate 2023F-2025F sales by 17% as we cut our projections for new stores to 100/150/300 in 2023F/2024F/2025F vs our previous assumption of 400 new stores per annum amid expected economic headwinds. That said, we believe pharmacy weathers the impact of economic slowdown relatively well as demand for medicines is relatively inelastic, which should allow LC to maintain margins and sales/store during a slowdown. We forecast LC’s gross margin at 23.0%/23.1% in 2023F/2024F (flat vs 9M 2022 GPM) vs our previous forecasts of 22.6%/22.7%, respectively. We forecast monthly sales/store to remain at VND1.1bn/1.2bn in 2023F/2024F. 9M 2022: Strong growth intact; pharmacy remains profitable Figure 1: FRT’s 9M 2022 results Source: FRT, VCSC. Note: * Online sales are included in FPT Shop’s revenue 2022F: We cut our NPAT-MI forecast by 29% due to iPhone supply disruption in Q4 2022 Figure 2: VCSC’s 2022 forecasts Source: VCSC 2023F: Weaker mobile outlook; pharmacy remains key growth driver Figure 3: VCSC’s 2023 forecasts Source: VCSC Valuation Our valuation approach for FRT consists of (1) a P/E valuation for the mobile business and (2) a 10-year DCF model for the pharmacy business. Source: VCSC’s forecasts Vietnam’s revised oil & gas law will encourage investment in new projects In 2021, PVN reviewed legal regulations in the oil & gas sector to identify obstacles in its operation. Vietnam’s current oil & gas law became effective in 1993 with amendments and supplements in 2000 and 2008, which are no longer suitable with the current reality. On November 14, 2022, Vietnam’s National Assembly approved the revised oil & gas law, which will be effective on July 1, 2023. Overall, we believe the revised law is slightly positive for the sector as it could facilitate exploration & production activities and accelerate domestic oil & gas projects such as Block B and Blue Whale. According to the management of PetroVietnam Technical Services (HNX: PVS), the new law changes would allow oil & gas operators to bolster their E&P activities through lowering the profit-sharing ratio of the host country and lowering taxes for critical projects. As such, the new law will help to accelerate domestic oil & gas projects and thus benefit the sector. Figure 35: New Oil & Gas Law vs old law Source: Vietnam’s National Assembly, VCSC We expect oil prices of USD75-90/bbl in 2023-2026 will support the development of new long-term domestic gas projects and total estimated capex of USD11bn Figure 36 is a list of energy projects in the pipeline that have a total capex of at least USD23.5bn. Figure 37 is a list of oil & gas related infrastructure projects that have a total capex of at least USD11bn. In H1 2022, the Government issued Decree 114/2021/ND-CP, which paves the wave for the O Mon III power plant (which uses gas from Block B) to get official development assistance (ODA) financing. In addition, Japanese investors in Block B agreed to receive no Government’s guarantee term. In Q4 2022, the Ministry of Industry and Trade (MoIT) concluded that Block B (1) will receive a final investment decision by June 2023 at the latest and (2) extract its first gas by Q4 2026 at the latest. We currently assume this gas field will extract its first gas in 2028. Gas prices from Block B are expected to not be competitive with imported LNG prices. However, we believe the development of Block B is critical as it ensures national energy security and creates work for oil & gas companies as well as jobs for Ca Mau Province. An earlier-than-expected groundbreaking for this project should imply upside risk for GAS, PVS and PVD. GAS is the biggest beneficiary of Block B, while PVS is set to be the earliest beneficiary. Delay in Blue Whale gas project. According to PVN, the progress of the Blue Whale gas project is slower than its plan due to adjustments in ExxonMobil’s investment strategy and barriers to investment. We currently assume this gas field to extract its first gas in 2030. We believe the approval of this project will benefit PVS, PVD and BSR; however, we see no impact for GAS. Delay in White Lion gas project. White Lion – Phase 2a extracted its first gas in June 2021. Context:
We expect a 2024F YoY sales volume forecast of -10%: BMP’s Q1 2024 revenue was weak, declining 30% YoY, fulfilling only 18% of the previous forecast. As such, we cut our 2024F sales volume to 78,300 tonnes (vs 95,600 tonnes previously), equivalent to a 10% YoY sales decline. We believe a further rebound in the real estate market for the remaining quarters of 2024F will drive a further volume rebound vs Q1. We expect 2024F EBIT margin to slide ~190bps from 2023’s high base, as we expect GPM to drop by 162bps to 39.4% from 2023’s record-high and SG&A/Sales to inch up 24bps as we expect BMP to slightly ramp up trade discounts amid weak demand. We note that 39.4% GPM expected in 2024F is the 2nd-highest point across BMP’s operations history. This is due to our view that input plastic costs will remain low this year as we continue to see slow recovery of Chinese demand, noting that China accounted for 44% of global demand for PVC resins – a critical input material for BMP. BMP remains our favorite defensive pick in the construction and materials sector in H2 2024. Supported by high 2024F earnings, strong cash generation, and a net-cash balance sheet, we expect BMP’s next-12M DPS of VND10,800, equivalent to a high 10.2% dividend yield. Further, as a low-beta stock, with 5- and 2-year Bloomberg adjusted betas of ~0.85, we believe BMP is a compelling defensive pick to guard against prevailing market volatilities and uncertainties. With a high cash dividend yield, we believe the recent share price decline has sent BMP’s valuation to an attractive level, with average a 2024-25F P/E of 9.9x. Our target price puts BMP’s average 2024-25F P/E at 10.1x, still lower than its 5-/10-year average P/Es of 10.9x/11.1x. 30 Company Overview Established in 1977, BMP is the pioneer of Vietnam’s plastic pipe industry. Currently, BMP’s largest shareholder (54.4% holdings) is Nawaplastic – a subsidiary of SCG Thailand. BMP - Weaker volumes but margins holding up better than expected

Context:
Strong growth driven by higher volume and profitability We maintain our BUY rating as we believe SAB’s solid momentum in gaining market share and increasing profitability will continue. We forecast SAB to generate double-digit earnings growth in 2022F-2024F due to a strong post-COVID-19 economic recovery and the company’s margin-enhancement initiatives. We raise our target price (TP) by 6% as we increase our aggregate 2022F-2024F NPAT-MI forecast by 5.3% mainly due to our 9.6% lower aggregate 2022F-2024F promotion expense forecast, which is partially offset by our projected increase in input costs. We expect revenue to increase by 35% YoY to VND36tn (USD1.5bn) with NPAT-MI of VND5.1tn (USD223mn; 40% YoY) in 2022F. Consequently, we expect 2022F core EPS to rise 46.2% YoY vs 39.6% previously, which is mainly driven by a post-COVID-19 demand rebound and slower pace of advertising & promotion (A&P) spending in 2022F-2024F. For 2021-2024F, we forecast an EPS CAGR of 22% (2.3 ppts higher than our previous projection) thanks to (1) the economy fully recovering, (2) SAB gaining market share along with an improvement of its product mix, and (3) the company’s margin enhancement initiatives. Our TP puts SAB’s 2022F/2023F P/E at 32x/27x vs a 5Y average peer median TTM P/E of 30x. Risks to our positive view: Market share loss to key rival; higher-than-expected marketing spending due to stiffening competition. Rebound of beer consumption thanks to economic recovery and expansion of market share. We raise our 2022F beer volume growth rate by 2 ppts to 21% YoY, which is mainly due to (1) traffic to retail and entertainment venues normalizing as a result of the end of social distancing restrictions and the recovery of consumer income in H1 2022, and (2) gaining market share through the improvement of the brand’s health and consideration along with the development of innovative products that are mainly focused on capturing the attention of young adult consumers. Gross profit margin is effectively shielded against increasing material prices. We forecast 2022F GPM at 28.7% (vs previously 29.9%) due to higher input prices. We expect the rise in aluminum and malting barley prices at respectively 37% YoY and 68% YoY on average in H1 2022 — which SAB already hedged in H1 2022 — to partially impact SAB’s gross margin in H2 2022F. However, we believe gross margin will supported by SAB’s solidified pricing power as well as its improved production efficiency. We also forecast SAB’s GPM in 2023F-2024F to improve due to lower input costs. More selective A&P spending to boost profitability. SAB maintained a generous A&P budget during COVID-19 as management wanted to prepare for a strong post-pandemic recovery. SAB has increased its optimization in A&P spending, resulting in H1 2022 A&P cost per revenue falling 40 bps YoY. Context:
Tăng trưởng mạnh trong quý 1/2022 nhờ mảng thủy điện và các trang trại điện gió mới CTCP Tập Đoàn PC1 (PC1) công bố KQKD quý 1/2022 với LNST sau lợi ích CĐTS đạt 133 tỷ đồng (+70% YoY) do lợi nhuận tăng mạnh từ mảng phát điện và mảng sản xuất trụ điện bù đắp cho lợi nhuận thấp hơn từ mảng xây lắp điện, không có hoạt động chuyển nhượng bất động sản và chi phí lãi vay tăng mạnh đến từ các trang trại điện gió mới đi vào hoạt động vào tháng 10/2021. Mảng phát điện có KQKD khả quan nhờ đóng góp lợi nhuận từ các trang trại điện gió mới và tăng trưởng lợi nhuận ổn định từ các nhà máy thủy điện nhờ lượng mưa đủ lớn. LNST sau lợi ích CĐTS quý 1/2022 của PC1 đã hoàn thành 17% dự báo cả năm của chúng tôi. Chúng tôi nhận thấy rủi ro điều chỉnh giảm dự báo lợi nhuận hiện tại của chúng tôi do chi phí lãi vay trong quý 1/2022 cao hơn dự kiến (chiếm 32% dự báo năm 2022 của chúng tôi) và khoản chi phí lãi vay tiềm năng đến từ lượng trái phiếu mới, dù cần thêm đánh giá chi tiết. PC1 dự kiến sẽ ghi nhận chi phí lãi vay từ trái phiếu/khoản vay bổ sung để tài trợ cho việc đầu tư vào các khu công nghiệp. Đáng chú ý, PC1 dự kiến huy động 1,2 nghìn tỷ đồng trái phiếu để tài trợ đầu tư vào các khu công nghiệp. Tính đến hiện tại, công ty đã phát hành 300 triệu đồng và đặt mục tiêu hoàn thành vào tháng 6. Chúng tôi nhận thấy khả năng điều chỉnh tăng dự báo lợi nhuận cả năm của chúng tôi cho năm 2023 và giai đoạn tiếp theo khi chúng tôi hiện không bao gồm đóng góp lợi nhuận từ các dự án khai thác niken trong dự báo và định giá của chúng tôi cho PC1. Lợi nhuận gộp của mảng sản xuất điện tăng gấp 4 lần trong quý 1/2022. Doanh thu và lợi nhuận gộp của mảng này phù hợp với kỳ vọng của chúng tôi, lần lượt hoàn thành 26% và 27% dự báo cả năm tương ứng của chúng tôi. Chúng tôi cho rằng KQKD này đến từ đóng góp lợi nhuận bổ sung từ 3 nhà máy điện gió đã đi vào hoạt động vào tháng 10/2021 và hoạt động ổn định của các nhà máy thủy điện nhờ lượng mưa đủ lớn. Mảng xây lắp điện có KQKD thấp trong quý 1/2021. Lợi nhuận gộp từ mảng xây lắp điện của PC1 giảm 69% YoY so với mức cơ sở cao vào quý 1/2021 nhờ lợi nhuận từ các hợp đồng EPC cho các dự án điện gió của bên thứ ba trong năm 2021. Lợi nhuận gộp của mảng này hoàn thành 8% dự báo cả năm của chúng tôi. Tuy nhiên, chúng tôi nhận thấy dự báo cả năm của chúng tôi sẽ không có những thay đổi đáng kể do mảng kinh doanh này thường tăng trưởng mạnh trong quý 2 và quý 4. Ngoài ra, PC1 đặt kế hoạch giá trị backlog ký mới đạt 7,5 nghìn tỷ đồng (+40% YoY) và doanh thu tiềm năng đạt 6 nghìn tỷ đồng trong năm 2022 – kế hoạch doanh thu này cao hơn 54% so với dự báo của chúng tôi (3,9 nghìn tỷ đồng).
