Context:
Tối ưu hóa chi phí G&A và chi phí tín dụng hỗ trợ lợi nhuận Ngân hàng TMCP Quân Đội (MBB) đã công bố kết quả kinh doanh hợp nhất 9 tháng đầu năm 2023 (9T 2023) với LNTT đạt 20,0 nghìn tỷ đồng (+10,0% YoY), hoàn thành 76% dự báo năm tài chính 2023 của chúng tôi và tương ứng LNTT quý 3/2023 đạt 7,3 nghìn tỷ đồng (+15,7% YoY; +17,0% QoQ). Nhìn chung, lợi nhuận của MBB phù hợp với dự báo cả năm của chúng tôi. Tăng trưởng tín dụng hợp nhất 9T 2023 đạt 13,7% so với hạn mức tín dụng hiện tại là 24,5%. Tăng trưởng tín dụng đến từ (1) tăng trưởng cho vay đạt 16,4% và (2) dư nợ trái phiếu doanh nghiệp của MBB giảm 13,2% so với quý 4/2022. Tính đến quý 3/2023, trái phiếu doanh nghiệp chiếm 7,1% tổng dư nợ tín dụng của MBB (so với 9,2% vào quý 4/2022). Tăng trưởng tiền gửi 9T 2023 đạt 8,1%. Tỷ lệ CASA quý 3/2023 giảm 1,1 điểm % QoQ xuống 36,0%, vẫn là mức cao nhất trong danh mục theo dõi của chúng tôi. NIM 9T 2023 đạt 5,31%, thấp hơn mức dự báo cả năm của chúng tôi là 5,55%. NIM quý 3/2023 đạt 5,03% (-1 điểm cơ bản QoQ & -74 điểm cơ bản YoY). Lợi suất tài sản sinh lãi (IEA) và chi phí vốn giảm lần lượt 40 điểm cơ bản QoQ và 41 điểm cơ bản QoQ trong quý 3/2023. Thu nhập ngoài lãi (NOII) 9T 2023 đạt 6,0 nghìn tỷ đồng (-19,0% YoY), hoàn thành 75% dự báo cả năm của chúng tôi. NOII quý 3/2023 đạt 2,3 nghìn tỷ đồng, tăng 15,7 % YoY và 8,4% QoQ. Tỷ lệ chi phí (CIR) hợp nhất 9T 2023 tăng 2 điểm % YoY lên 31,0% so với dự báo cả năm của chúng tôi là 31,5%. Chi phí HĐKD (OPEX) 9T 2023 đã hoàn thành 70,0% dự báo cả năm của chúng tôi và thấp hơn kỳ vọng của chúng tôi do chi phí nhân viên giảm 7,3% YoY. Trong quý 3/2023, tỷ lệ nợ xấu hợp nhất tăng 45 điểm cơ bản QoQ đạt 1,89% (so với dự báo cả năm của chúng tôi là 1,63%) trong khi tỷ lệ nợ nhóm 2 giảm 62 điểm cơ bản xuống 2,97% (so với dự báo cả năm của chúng tôi là 2,50%). Chi phí tín dụng quy năm trong 9T 2023 đạt 1,20% (so với 1,75% vào năm 2022) và tỷ lệ xử lý nợ bằng dự phòng trên tổng dư nợ 9T 2023 đạt 1,04% (so với 0,96% vào năm 2022). Context:
Q1 2022 profit surge driven by compensation income GVR announced Q1 2022 results with revenue flat YoY at VND4.9tn (USD213mn) while NPAT-MI surged 29% YoY to VND1.1tn (USD46mn). In Q1 2022, the top line was mainly driven by a 10% YoY increase in natural rubber latex revenue to VND3tn (USD130mn), accounting for 61% of GVR’s total revenue in the quarter and offset by 41% YoY decline in rubber product revenue to VND475bn (USD21mn) — accounting for 10% of GVR’s total revenue in Q1 2022. Meanwhile, the bottom-line YoY surge in Q1 2022 was mainly thanks to compensation income from VSIP III IP of VND280bn (USD12mn) from land conversion in the quarter vs only VND7bn (USD305,000) of compensation income in Q1 2021. GVR’s Q1 2022 revenue and NPAT-MI completed 16% and 20% of our full-year forecasts, respectively. As Q1 is normally low season for the natural rubber segment and divestment plan finalization, we anticipate no major risks to our current forecasts, pending a fuller review. Natural rubber latex revenue was broadly in line with our expectation, while GPM was higher than expected. In Q1 2022, GVR’s natural rubber latex revenue jumped 10% YoY to VND3tn (USD130mn), representing 14% of our full-year forecast and broadly in line with our current forecasts as Q1 revenue typically accounted for ~15% of full-year revenue on average in 2019 - 2021. We estimate natural rubber latex GPM expanded 14 ppts YoY to 38% in Q1 2022 vs 24% in Q1 2021 and our 2022F GPM forecast of 30%. We note that GVR has not announced its volume and ASP breakdown, but we believe the growth of natural rubber latex revenue in Q1 2022 was mainly driven by ASP increases that leading to YoY GPM expansion in the quarter. The rubber product segment was below our current forecast. In Q1 2022, revenue from rubber products plunged 41% YoY to VND475bn (USD21mn), representing ~14% of our full-year forecast. This segment mainly includes rubber glove production. We believe the weak performance of this segment was mainly due to COVID-19 disruptions subsiding and increased competition from other local manufacturers. Compensation income recognition drove profits in Q1 2022 and were mainly derived from rubber land conversion to the VSIP III IP from GVR’s 67%-owned subsidiary Phuoc Hoa Rubber (HOSE: PHR). We note that as PHR and VSIP III have obtained all necessary approval for land conversion, PHR now can recognize compensation income from land conversion based on actual cash payment from VSIP III. However, the payment timeline has not been finalized, per PHR. GVR’s Q1 2022 results Source: GVR, VCSC

In 2015, the petrol distribution segment, which accounted for ~83% of OIL’s profit, fueled net profit thanks to a policy change in which the Government raised regulated costs per liter from VND860 to VND1,050 for gasoline and VND950 for diesel. However, net profit declined in 2016-2017 due to lower profit from petrol distribution segment following the oil price’s plunge in 2016 and lower profit of PVOil Laos in 2017. In 2018, the petroleum distribution segment recovered thanks to 6.1% COCO sales volume growth, however, a significant increase in corporate income tax expense, and lower profit from crude entrusted export service and petroleum processing segments sent net profit lower. Strong financial capacity with huge cash on hand and negative net gearing ratio. OIL has strong financial capacity with year-end balances of cash and short-term investments of ~USD270 million-USD320 million and net gearing ratios of negative 40%-55% in the past five years. Company guidance for 2019 and H1 2019 results 2019 guidance: OIL conservatively targets 2019 revenue to decline 14.0% YoY and NPAT before MI to drop 11.9% YoY. The revenue decline is due to an oil price assumption of USD65/barrel, -8.7% YoY. Meanwhile, we attribute the conservative net profit guidance to: 1) OIL prepared its 2019 plan in Q4 2018 when the petroleum market was very unfavorable as the oil price plunged and 2) lower volume in the crude entrusted service segment due to a decline in oil production volume. This is despite a 3% increase in petroleum sales volume and a 7% increase in COCO sales volume. H1 2019 results: OIL posted H1 revenue of VND38.6tn (USD1.6bn, +19.4%YoY) and net profit of VND194bn (USD8.3mn, -35.5%YoY). The weak H1 was due to challenging market conditions in Q1 and late May/early June, coupled with the Government’s overuse of the stabilization fund in Q1. This was partly offset by an inventory provision reversal of VND80bn (USD3.4mn). We note that there was significant improvement in Q2 with net profit of VND163bn (USD7mn) vs Q1 net profit of VND32bn (USD1.4mn). H1 2019 NPAT before MI achieved 77% of OIL’s full year guidance. The expected 4.7% consumption growth comes from: 1. Robust industrial production will lead to an increase in goods transported by trucks and ships, as well as growth in fuel oil and diesel oil consumption for machines and factories. 2. Context:
Solid H1 thanks to COCO sales volume and domestic input source from Nghi Son PLX’s H1 2019 revenue declined 5.1%YoY while reported NPAT-MI was up 12.6%YoY. The revenue decline was mainly due to average gasoline and diesel price decreases of 3.4% and 0.9%, respectively. Meanwhile, we attribute the solid NPAT-MI growth to 1) COCO sales volume growth of 3.9% (the highest margin segment), 2) a smaller forex loss thanks to a stable exchange and domestic input source from Nghi Son refinery (NSR) and 3) a VND440bn (USD19mn) inventory provision write-back in H1 2019. H1 revenue and reported NPAT-MI completed 47.4% and 56.0% of our respective full year forecasts and were in line with our expectations. Therefore, we do not foresee material changes to our 2019 forecasts. H1 total sales volume grew 3.0%YoY, mainly driven by COCO sales volume of 3.9%YoY. Per PLX, COCO sales volume growth has been gradually improving with growth of 5.5% in June 2019 along with expected higher growth in July. Therefore, we see minimal downside risk to our COCO sales assumption of 6.0%. Petroleum market conditions in Q2 2019 were better than in Q1 2019, although not as favorable as in Q2 2018. Excluding the impact of the inventory write-back in Q1, PLX’s Q2 core business witnessed an improvement vs Q1 (+26% QoQ) as the Government allowed the petroleum price to increase in accordance with international prices, which differed from when the Government controlled the petroleum price in Q1 due to an 8.4% increase in electricity price. Having said that, Q2 2019 NPAT-MI still declined 3.4%YoY off a high base in Q2 2018 when petroleum price movements were even more favorable for PLX (see Figure 2 on page 2). Less pressure on PLX’s stabilization fund. Q2 saw solid performance despite PLX’s negative stabilization fund of VND356bn (USD15.3mn) at end of April 2019 after the Government overused stabilization funds to prevent petroleum prices from rising in Q1. The stabilization fund at PLX turned from negative to positive at VND54bn (USD2.3mn) by the end of June 2018, and then increased to VND470bn (USD20mn) as of August 1, 2019. This shift implies less pressure on stabilization fund in Q3. PLX successfully sold 20 million treasury shares in July 2019. According data from HOSE, PLX sold 20 million shares (equivalent a 1.7% stake after the sale) from July 2, 2019 to July 31, 2019. Proceeds (VND1.3tn/USD56mn at the current price of VND65,000/share) will be used to finance core business expansion. After this transaction, PLX’s treasury shares have been reduced to 103 million shares (~8% stake after the sale). Context:
Công bố kế hoạch mở rộng quỹ đất tham vọng CTCP Phát triển Đô thị Kinh Bắc (KBC) đã tổ chức ĐHCĐ vào ngày 29/06/2020 tại tỉnh Bắc Ninh. Cổ đông đã thông qua kế hoạch cho năm 2020 theo 2 kịch bản. Theo kịch bản cơ sở, KBC đặt mục tiêu tổng thu nhập (bao gồm doanh thu, thu nhập tài chính và thu nhập khác) đạt 3,2 nghìn tỷ đồng (-3% YoY) và LNST đạt 816 tỷ đồng (-22% YoY). Theo kịch bản tích cực hơn, công ty đặt mục tiêu tổng thu nhập đạt 3,6 nghìn tỷ đồng (+9% YoY) và LNST đạt 1 nghìn tỷ đồng (-3,9% YoY). Mục tiêu doanh thu theo kịch bản cơ sở của KBC cao hơn 12% so với dự báo của chúng tôi, trong khi mục tiêu LNST theo kịch bản cơ sở thấp hơn 2% so với dự báo của chúng tôi. Chúng tôi cho rằng chênh lệnh trong doanh thu đến từ dự phóng doanh số bán đất lớn hơn của KBC; tuy nhiên, mục tiêu LNST thấp hơn cho thấy quan điểm của ban lãnh đạo đối với khả năng sinh lời của các dự án là thận trọng hơn so với kỳ vọng của chúng tôi. Cổ đông ủy quyền cho HĐQT của KBC quyết định kế hoạch cổ tức năm tài chính 2019. Nếu dòng tiền mặt ròng của KBC đạt kỳ vọng, cổ tức tiền mặt cho năm tài chính 2019 sẽ ở mức tối thiểu 10% trên mệnh giá, hoặc 1.000 đồng/CP (tương ứng lợi suất cổ tức 7,2%). Chúng tôi hiện dự báo không có cổ tức tiền mặt cho năm tài chính 2019. KCN Quang Châu và Nam Sơn Hạp Lĩnh (NSHL) sẽ là các yếu tố đóng góp chính cho năm 2020 khi ban lãnh đạo chia sẻ danh sách các khách thuê tiềm năng cho 2 KCN trong năm 2020, bao gồm ít nhất 105ha cho KCN Quang Châu và 73 ha cho KCN NSHL. Trong danh sách này, có 2 khách thuê lớn từ Trung Quốc, bao gồm (1) một công ty sản xuất linh kiện điện thoại có kế hoạch thuê 62 ha tại KCN NSHL và (2) một nhà sản xuất xe đạp điện có kế hoạch thuê 50 ha tại KCN Quang Châu. Mở rộng KCN Quang Châu nhằm tận dụng xu hướng tăng trưởng dài hạn. KBC có kế hoạch đền bù thêm 90 ha tại KCN Quang Châu và chuyển đổi 90 ha đất sạch tại KĐT Quang Châu thành đất KCN; do đó, KCN sẽ được mở rộng thêm 180 ha. Tính đến cuối quý 1/2020, quỹ đất có thể cho thuê tại KCN Quang Châu là 61ha. KCN NSHL hiện vẫn đang chờ đợi phê duyệt chính thức nhằm ghi nhận doanh số bán đất. trong khi KBC đã hoàn thành giải phóng mặt bằng cho 66ha quỹ đất có thể cho thuê (chiếm khoảng 35% tổng diện tích đất có thể cho thuê), công ty vẫn cần phê duyệt chính thức cho tổng diện tích KCN 300 ha – vốn được điều chỉnh từ mức trước đấy là 432,5ha - nhằm ghi nhận doanh số bán đất. Ban lãnh đạo chia sẽ kế hoạch phát triển quỹ đất tham vọng tại các tỉnh thành khác, bao gồm Hải Dương tại miền Bắc và Long An tại miền Nam. Ban lãnh đạo chia sẻ rằng KBC hiện đang nhắm đến mục tiêu phát triển 3 KCN tại Hải Dương, bao gồm một KCN gần KCN Tràng Duệ tại Hải Phòng. Tất

Context:
Near-term gas projects slow; LNG a huge opportunity We raise our TP by ~10% as 1) we lift 2020F earnings by ~20% given higher gas prices for industrial parks and 2) factor the Son My LNG project into our forecast from 2025F, outweighing 3) the negative impact of trimming 2021-2024F earnings by ~10% on average due to slow progress of the Sao Vang – Dai Nguyet (SV-DN) and White Lion – phase 2 (WLP2) projects. We downgrade our recommendation from BUY to OUTPERFORM as GAS’s share price has increased by ~30% in the past two months. We forecast 2020F NPAT-MI to drop by 43.3% YoY, mainly due to fuel oil (FO) price and gas volume declines of 36.9% and 5.4% YoY, respectively. We estimate an EPS CAGR of 7.8% over 2019-2030F, driven by more profit coming from the transportation segment outweighing declining profits from the gas trading segment over the long term when cheap gas fields run out of reserves. We project profit from the transportation segment to double and contribute ~70% to GAS’s net profit by 2025F vs ~40% in 2019. GAS has strong financial capacity with USD1.4bn of cash on hand and net gearing of -57.4%. Upside risks: faster-than-expected progress of SV-DN; continuing profit recognition from increased gas prices for the under take-or-pay (ToP) segment in 2020; changes of reference oil price in the gas price formula. Downside risk: lower-than-expected oil prices. We keep our average Brent and FO price at USD35/bbl and USD228/ton for 2020F, before recovering to USD60/bbl and USD299/ton from 2021F, respectively, as stated in our March 24, 2020 Energy Sector Update. Average Brent oil price in Q1 dropped by 18% YoY to USD52/bbl while average FO price declined by 31.8% YoY to USD268/ton — in line with our forecasts. Higher-than-expected gas price for industrial parks in H1 2020. Following Q1 results, we learned the average gas price for industrial parks in Q1 was USD8.7/MMBTU — ~40% higher than our previous forecast. As the gas price for industrial parks is negotiated twice a year, we expect it will follow the market price mechanism (~95% of medium fuel oil, MFO) and fall to USD5.4/MMBTU in H2 2020. Therefore, we lift our 2020F average gas price for industrial parks by 17.8% to USD6.6/MMBTU, which is the underlying reason for lifting 2020F NPAT-MI by 20.1%. Lower gas sales volume outlook for the near term. We revise down 2020F sales volume by 4% due to a technical issue at the Block 11.2 gas field. We also cut gas sales volume for 2021-2024 by 7.8% on average due to the slow progress of SV-DN and WLP2 projects. We assume SV-DN and WLP2 will contribute their first gas in H2 2021 and 2024 vs beginning 2021/2022, respectively. We factor the Son My LNG terminal into GAS’s long-term forecast. GAS is hastening the Son My LNG terminal project’s development. This project’s feasibility study report was submitted to PVN in August 2019; recently, management has been confident in getting approval. We factor this project into our forecast from 2025F, which lifts 2025-2030F sales volume by ~23% and NPAT-MI by ~10%. Định giá đắt đỏ sau đợt tăng giá theo đà phục hồi của giá dầu Chúng tôi giữ nguyên khuyến nghị KÉM KHẢ QUAN dành cho CTCP Khoan và Dịch vụ Khoan Dầu khí (PVD) khi tâm lý cải thiện do giá dầu thô hồi phục không đủ bù đắp cho giá thuê giàn và hiệu suất hoạt động trong quý 1 thấp hơn dự kiến. Tuy nhiên, khuyến nghị của chúng tôi có độ nhạy cao đối với giá dầu thô. Mức giá tăng 10USD/thùng so với giả định cơ sở của chúng tôi trong suốt kỳ dự báo sẽ tương ứng với khuyến nghị KHẢ QUAN. Cả giá thuê giàn tự nâng và hiệu suất hoạt động đều giảm mạnh trong quý 1. Giá thuê ngày của PVD giảm 50% trong Quý 1 trong khi hiệu suất hoạt động đội giàn khoan giảm 75 điểm cơ bản, phù hợp với diễn biến khu vực Đông Nam Á nói chung. PVD cho biết có hai giàn khoan tự nâng (PVDIII và PVDVI) đã ngừng khoan cho Murphy Oil và KNOC kể từ giữa tháng 05/2016, nên hiện chỉ có một giàn khoan tự nâng (PVDI) đang hoạt động. Do đó chúng tôi điều chỉnh giảm giả định giá thuê cũng như hiệu suất hoạt động cho giàn tự nâng trong năm 2016. Chúng tôi điều chỉnh giảm hiệu suất hoạt động trung bình của các giàn khoan tự nâng cả năm từ 49% xuống 39%, trong đó dự báo mới thấp hơn nhiều so với con số 88,6% đạt được năm 2015. Chúng tôi cũng giảm 20% giả định giá thuê ngày trung bình để phù hợp với mức hiện nay của khu vực là 80.000USD/ngày, thấp hơn đến 37,4% so với năm 2015. Giàn tiếp trợ khoan (TAD) tiếp tục là nguồn thu nhập ổn định của PVD. Chúng tôi giữ tất cả các giả định dành cho giàn khoan TAD, với hiệu suất hoạt động bằng năm ngoái 96%, và giá thuê ngày trung bình giảm nhẹ 7% xuống 180.000USD/ngày trong năm 2016. Thay đổi cách thức ghi nhận khấu hao sẽ làm giảm bớt áp lực đối với lợi nhuận. Như đã đề cập trong báo cáo ĐHCĐ, các điều chỉnh về phương pháp ghi nhận khấu hao sẽ khiến chi phí khấu hao năm 2016 giảm một nửa (khoảng 30 triệu USD), và điều này đã được phản ánh vào KQLN Quý 1. Dù khấu hao giảm, chúng tôi vẫn dự báo lợi nhuận năm 2016 giảm 69% so với 2015. Context:
Solid volume growth and tax change boost earnings In H1 2019, SCS achieved revenue growth of 15% YoY to VND362bn (USD15.7mn) and NPAT-MI growth of 21% YoY to VND240bn (USD10.4mn). We attribute this top and bottom-line growth to continued strong international cargo volume growth – SCS’s key earnings driver – which was up 12% YoY in H1 2019. H1 2019 revenue and NPAT-MI completed 48% and 51% of our respective full-year forecasts. Although these results are broadly in line with our expectations, pending a more extensive review, we see upside risk to our 2019 forecast as runway maintenance at Tan Son Nhat International Airport (SGN) is no longer expected to take place this year (see our AGM note for ACV here for more details). We continue to be bullish on SCS’s long-term outlook as we forecast it will deliver top-line 2018-2023 CAGR of 19% with a strong NPM of 63%-75% and ROE of 48%-60%. We expect this to be driven by (1) vibrant air cargo transport demand in Vietnam, (2) SCS’s duopoly market position with high barriers to entry at SGN and (3) its 90% cash conversion ratio due to low capex needs of USD8mn over 2019-2023 along with no debt. Slower Q2 2019 partially eroded strong international cargo volume growth in Q1 2019. In H1 2019, SCS posted strong international cargo volume growth of 12% YoY compared to 6.3% YoY in H1 2018. We believe this strong growth was thanks to new large customers onboarded in Q2 2018, including Japan and AirBridgeCargo Airlines. However, this also led to a high base in Q2 2018. In Q2 2019, SCS only posted international cargo volume growth of 8.3% YoY vs 16% YoY in Q1 2019. Lower effective tax rate boosted the bottom line. In H1 2019, SCS’s effective corporate income tax rate decreased to 6.3% from 10.9% in H1 2018. As we previously noted (see our daily note from May 23, 2019 here), SCS’s corporate income tax rate was lowered in line with Decree 218/2013/ND-CP, which implied that SCS’s air cargo terminal business should benefit from tax incentives as it is a relatively new project. We believe SCS will be reimbursed for overpaid taxes over 2014-2018 in later quarters. Related-party loan to GMD fully extinguished in direct response to shareholder objections. In what we view as a positive development for SCS’s corporate governance, the company’s Q2 2019 balance sheet shows that its loan (VND103bn/USD4.5mn as at the end of Q1 2019, ~10% of SCS’s total assets) to its largest shareholder Gemadept (HSX: GMD) was fully extinguished during the quarter. While shareholders were not concerned with GMD’s repayment ability and the loan generated interest income for SCS, they saw this as a negative for SCS’s corporate governance given it was out of line with the company’s core business as well as the related party. SCS’s Q2 2019 and H1 2019 results Source: SCS and VCSC

Although growth tempo moderated to an 8.3[3D[K 8.3% volume increase in Q2, the removal of a VND103bn loan owed to related [K party Gemadept addressed a key corporate governance issue [S5]. Maintaining[11D[K Maintaining a forward-looking perspective, the long-term bull case remains [K viable with an estimated 19% revenue CAGR extending into 2023 [S6], underpi[7D[K underpinned by high margin potential (63%-75%) and an ROE trajectory of 48%[3D[K 48%-60% [S7]. Capital discipline is further evident in projected capex of o[1D[K only USD8mn over the coming five years [S8], suggesting a highly efficient [K operational model, even as runway maintenance at Tan Son Nhat International[13D[K International Airport poses near-term execution risks.

seats, accounts for around half of TCM’s export revenue and plans to maintain this level of contribution. We believe TCM’s P/E valuation is expensive as TCM is currently trading at TTM P/E of 9.8x — a 32% premium to peers — although its current high earnings are mainly driven by temporary demand surge for new products amid COVID-19 and our projected EPS CAGR of 5.5% over 2019-2025F. Upside risks: Successful entry into new export markets; successful development of real estate projects; recovery of bad debt from Sears and successful M&A with current outsourcing partners. Downside risks: Slowdown in productivity improvement; drop in demand from TCM’s key export markets. Company Overview: TCM is one of the largest garment and textile companies in Vietnam. It operates a fully integrated chain from yarn spinning to garment manufacturing. South Korean Conglomerate E-Land Group is a strategic investor. Its main export markets include the US, Japan and South Korea. VJC – Flying through tough year, but recovery will take long time 29 We forecast that VJC’s bottom line to return to the pre-COVID level in 2024. We project VJC’s volume (in terms of RPK) to return to 2019’s level within 2024F and the airline will have to lower international airfare as we expect VJC to compete with lower prices during the early recovery phase of international aviation. Domestic market will drive the initial recovery, while the comeback of international aviation could take longer. International flights were allowed in Vietnam starting in September; however, the restrictions and quarantine requirements remain strict. Moreover, international tourists are still unable to fly to Vietnam. We expect international flight restrictions will be relaxed in H2 2021 when we believe COVID-19 vaccines will make progress. We assume that VJC’s international volume (in terms of RPK) in 2021/22/23/24F will equal 29%/81%/91%/102% of the level in 2019. Upside /(downside) risks: faster/(slower) resumption of international flights; lower/(higher) oil prices. Company Overview: VietJet Air is the largest low-cost carrier in Vietnam. Since its first flight in 2011, VJC has capitalized on Vietnam’s domestic aviation boom to reach 41% domestic market share in 2019. The budget airline had 79 aircraft serving 139 routes at the end of 2019.

VJC, conversely, appears better positioned for a cyclic[6D[K cyclical rebound, driven by a clear path to international volume normalizat[10D[K normalization [S8]. The divergence in sector dynamics—textile demand versus[6D[K versus aviation recovery—creates a contrasting risk-return profile between [K the two firms.

Context:
Well-contained credit quality; strong NII growth We increase our target price (TP) by 9.6% to VND37,700/share and maintain our BUY rating for MBB due to (1) raising our aggregate net income forecast for 2021-2025 by 6.6% relative to our previous forecast and (2) the positive effect of rolling over our TP to end-2022F. Our TP implies a 2022F P/B of 1.84x. We cut our 2021F net income by 3.7% to VND12.8tn (USD558mn; +49.0% YoY) vs our previous forecast because of (1) a 25.7% increase in our provision expense assumption that outweighs (2) a 4.4% increase in pre-provision operating profit (PPOP). We maintain our assumption for 2021F loan growth at 20% YoY, which is supported by (1) a boost in mortgage lending from a partnership made with Novaland (HOSE: NVL) in January 2021, (2) growth in loans for renewable energy projects amid favorable Government policies and (3) sustainable growth in retail loans driven by the deployment of digital solutions such as online loans. We trim our 2021F deposit growth assumption from 15% in our previous forecast to 13% despite 9M deposit growth of 10.6% as we believe the current low-interest rate environment will put pressure on this funding source. Upside risks: Higher-than-expected increase in the retail lending portion of the consolidated loan book; better-than-expected performance of restructured loans may require less provision charges. Downside risks: CASA ratio downside risk and execution risk in MCredit’s development; prolonged COVID-19 pandemic leading to higher NPLs and failure to contain credit cost; pressure to reduce lending interest rate to support economic growth. Despite the latest round of loan rate cuts, 9M 2021 NII was in line with our expectation, completing 74% of our 2021F forecast thanks to its improvement on COF. We believe the pressure on IEA yield will ease toward the end of the year with 2021F NIM possibly delivering a 42-bp YoY expansion. We project credit costs to peak in 2021F before gradually declining in the next two years. According to our estimation based on the bank’s disclosure, restructured loans edged up from VND2.7tn in Q2 2021 to VND3.4tn in Q3 2021 (but not surpassing 2020’s peak of VND5.1tn in Q3 2020). 9M 2021 provision expenses surged 43.5%YoY as MBB fully provisioned for restructured loans instead of allocating them over three years — similar to ACB. Therefore, we lift our forecast for 2021F provision expenses by 25.7%. In our view, MBB’s credit quality was well contained as the 9M 2021 LLR of MBB Group increased to 232.8% vs 134.1% at end-FY2020, representing the highest LLR among our private bank coverage universe and surpassing TCB. We view MBB’s current valuation as attractive given its solid growth while maintaining top credit quality. In addition, we continue to apply the same forward P/B multiple of 1.9x for MBB that we apply to ACB. In 9M 2021, MBB outperformed ACB in terms of lending and deposit gathering activities (12.8% and 10.6% vs 8.0% and 3.6%, respectively). Furthermore, MBB maintained the second highest CASA ratio among banks under our coverage in the last four quarters — only after TCB. 9M 2021 Recap: Strong NII growth counteracts provision expense burden Figure 1: MBB’s consolidated 9M 2021 results Source: MBB, VCSC. Units are in VND bn unless otherwise stated — *Net fee income includes FX income; **CASA volume includes demand and margin deposits. 2021F Outlook: Strong NII growth supports bottom-line growth Figure 2: VCSC’s 2021 forecasts Source: VCSC. Units are in VND bn unless otherwise stated — *Net fee income includes FX income; **CASA volume includes demand and margin deposits; we assume a capital raising plan via a private placement

Context:
đồng cho 12 dự án hóa chất và hóa dầu trong giai đoạn 2023-2030, được đề cập trong Báo cáo ĐHCĐ của DPM ngày 23/06/2022. Tuy nhiên, tiến độ của các dự án này đã chậm hơn so với kế hoạch. Ban lãnh đạo cho biết DPM đang thực hiện 3 dự án: 1) nhà máy sản xuất Soda Ash (công suất 200.000 tấn/năm & vốn đầu tư 2.300 tỷ đồng), 2) nhà máy oxy già (H2O2) (công suất 40.000 tấn/năm & vốn đầu tư 800 tỷ đồng), và 3) nhà máy melamine (công suất 40.000 tấn/năm & vốn đầu tư 3.322 tỷ đồng). 1) Nhà máy sản xuất Soda Ash: DPM đang tiến hành nghiên cứu nhà máy sản xuất soda ash. Nếu dự án này được phê duyệt, DPM sẽ xây dựng nhà máy này trong giai đoạn 2024-2026 và sẵn sàng đi vào hoạt động từ năm 2027. Soda Ash được sử dụng trong nhiều quy trình công nghiệp để sản xuất các vật liệu như thủy tinh, giấy và xà phòng. 2) Nhà máy H2O2: DPM đã hoàn thành nghiên cứu khả thi cho nhà máy H2O2. Trong sản xuất công nghiệp, H2O2 chủ yếu được dùng làm chất tẩy trắng vải và giấy. 3) Nhà máy melamine: Ban lãnh đạo ước tính nhu cầu melamine trong nước là 15.000 tấn. Do đó, DPM đang tìm kiếm khách hàng xuất khẩu để đảm bảo tiêu thụ 25.000 tấn còn lại. Melamine chủ yếu được sử dụng làm lớp phủ, bộ lọc, chất kết dính và bộ dụng cụ bàn ăn. Ba dự án này có thể được phát triển dựa trên chuỗi giá trị sản xuất urê hiện có của DPM. Tuy nhiên, chúng tôi vẫn chưa đưa đóng góp tiềm năng của các dự án này vào dự báo của chúng tôi. Ngoài ra, chúng tôi không chắc chắn về thời gian xây dựng thực tế và đóng góp lợi nhuận trong những năm đầu của các dự án này do các dự án này hiện đang ở giai đoạn đầu của quá trình phát triển. ĐHCĐ bầu Ông Hồ Quyết Thắng và Ông Nguyễn Ngọc Anh làm thành viên HĐQT. Ông Hồ Quyết Thắng hiện là Chủ tịch HĐQT Tổng Công ty Bảo dưỡng - Sửa chữa Dầu khí Việt Nam và có 27 năm kinh nghiệm trong lĩnh vực dầu khí và điện. Ông Nguyễn Ngọc Anh hiện đang công tác tại Ban Kiểm soát nội bộ của Tập đoàn Dầu khí Việt Nam và có 15 năm kinh nghiệm trong lĩnh vực dầu khí. Hai thành viên mới này thay thế ông Louis T. Nguyễn và ông Lê Cự Tân, và ông Lê Cự Tân tiếp tục giữ chức vụ Tổng Giám đốc của DPM.

Giá theo hợp đồng mua bán điện cho Nhà máy Phả Lại 1 sẽ chỉ giảm nhẹ, giá mục tiêu không đổi Chúng tôi giữ nguyên giá mục tiêu dành cho CTCP Nhiệt điện Phả Lại (PPC) tại mức 21.700VND/cổ phiếu (vì việc bao gồm định giá theo phương pháp P/E đã bù đắp lại cho việc giá tính theo phương pháp chiết khấu dòng tiền giảm do phần bù rủi ro thị trường cao hơn) và tiếp tục đưa ra khuyến nghị MUA. LNST 2015 chuẩn hóa vượt dự báo của chúng tôi 14%. Chủ yếu vì chi phí bảo dưỡng thấp hơn 20% so với ước tính của chúng tôi, bù đắp cho việc tổng sản lượng thấp hơn 5% so với ước tính của chúng tôi. Chi phí bảo dưỡng thấp vì: (1) PPC bảo trì một tổ máy thay vì ba; (2) Việc tham gia của REE trong khâu quản lý đã giúp giảm chi phí bảo dưỡng. Dự phòng cho Nhà máy điện Quảng Ninh là quá thận trọng. PPC đã dự phòng thêm 322 tỷ đồng cho khoản đầu tư này trong năm 2015, nâng giá trị dự phòng lũy kế lên đến 406 tỷ đồng, tương đương 50% giá trị đầu tư ban đầu. Việc dự phòng mạnh cho Nhà máy điện Quảng Ninh (QNPP) không phù hợp với Chuẩn mực Báo cáo Tài chính Quốc tế (IFRS) và không hợp lý nếu nhìn vào lợi nhuận hoạt động sản xuất điện cũng như triển vọng kinh doanh của QNPP. PPC mua QNPP với giá 10,000 đồng, trong khi lỗ lũy kế của nhà máy điện này hoàn toàn là lỗ chênh lệch tỷ giá chưa thực hiện. Rủi ro lớn nhất của PPC liên quan đến việc thương lượng lại hợp đồng mua bán điện cho nhà máy điện Phả Lại 1 có thể sẽ không lớn như lo ngại. PPC ước tính trong kịch bản xấu nhất, giá theo hợp đồng mua bán điện cho nhà máy Phả Lại 1 sẽ chỉ giảm nhẹ khoảng 18VND/kWh, thấp hơn 1% so với giá cũ (tương đương mức giảm 7% trong phần cố định của công thức của hợp đồng mua bán điện). Chúng tôi ước tính mức giảm giá này sẽ khiến lợi nhuận chuẩn hóa của PPC chỉ giảm 5%. ThờI điểm ký kết hợp đồng mua bán điện mới vẫn chưa được công bố. Chúng tôi vẫn cho rằng PPC có thể trả cổ tức tại mức 1.500VND/cổ phiếu cho năm 2015 và cổ tức có thể tăng lên 2.000VND/cổ phiếu trong tương lai. Tuy LNST theo báo cáo của PPC thấp hơn 27% so với dự báo của chúng tôi do khoản dự phòng dành cho nhà máy điện Quảng Ninh, một thành viên HĐQT của PPC cho biết chắc chắn PPC sẽ trả mức cổ tức trên (tỷ lệ trả cổ tức 105%) vì PPC có dự trữ tiền mặt lớn và Tổng công ty phát điện 2 yêu cầu mức cổ tức cao. Chúng tôi ước tính PPC có thể tăng mạnh cổ tức lên 2.000VND/cổ phiếu trong tương lai nếu đồng yen Nhật và đồng USD không tăng giá quá 5% so với tiền đồng Việt Nam. Mức cổ tức này cho thấy tỷ lệ trả cổ tức sẽ lên đạt đỉnh tại mức 87% trong năm 2017. Mức chiết khấu 60% của PPC so với các công ty điện khác trong khu vực, và lợi suất cổ tức lên đến 11,9% sẽ bù đắp cho áp lực về giá do bị loại khỏi danh mục

REE (158MW), PC1 (43MW), and TV2 (~20MW, adjusted for ownership). Approved Execution Plan of PDP VIII in April 2024 to mainly benefit renewable power stocks Renewable power projects by companies 51 The Government released Decree 80 on July 3, 2024, on the DPPA mechanism. We expect the guidance of the DPPA to be released in H1 2025, providing sufficient details to make investments into renewable power again. The DPPA creates a win-win scenario, fostering renewable energy investments while supporting end-users in achieving cost stability and sustainability targets. We expect Direct PPA (DPPA)guidance to be released in H1 2025 52 Source: MoIT, Vietcap (*EPTC: EVN’s Power Trading Corporation, NSMO: National Power System and Market Operation) Draft DPPA mechanism, case 2 Renewable power plant Large end-user EVN (EPTC/NSMO)* Electricity retailer Retail power agreement PPA Contract for Difference (CfD) Spot price + service fees (for transmission & distribution, management of Vietnam’s Competitive Generation Market (VCGM), etc) Spot price + service fees Spot price If CfD price > spot price, end-users pays the difference If CfD price < spot price, power plant pays the difference Electricity flow Power agreement payment flow CfD payment flow 53 Source: EVN, Vietcap We anticipate higher new ceiling prices for renewable power in 2025 from the MoIT, incentivizing investors beyond transitional plant rates. With a projected 5% increase over transitional project prices, we estimate project IRRs at ~10%-12%, matching traditional power sources' returns (including medium and large-scale hydropower and thermal plants). We also expect this to be a competitive bidding/auction mechanism with ceiling prices. We expect a new pricing mechanism for renewable power in 2025 54 Source: Companies shown, Vietcap forecasts (* Based on companies’ guidance and have not been incorporated in our projections) Most companies under our coverage aim to significantly expand their renewable power capacity, primarily focusing on wind power, through self-development, M&A, and strategic partnerships. REE, HDG, and PC1 adopt a build-to-operate strategy, while GEX pursues a build-to-sell approach. We project REE/HDG to add approximately 750/600 MW, respectively, of solar and wind power capacity during 2024-2028F, 4x and 5x increases over the period. Renewable power capacity expansion outlook of power companies 55 Source: Amended Electricity Law (Draft), Vietcap On November 30, 2024, the National Assembly approved the Amended Electricity Law (AEL), introducing the following framework for offshore wind to attract investment. Amended Electricity Law establishes a clearer legal framework for offshore wind development Vietnam’s offshore wind key highlights 56 56 PC1 – Power construction and hydro to drive 2025 earnings jump Company Overview PC1 has 50 years of experience in erecting and installing power transmission lines & substations in Vietnam. Its key businesses include 1) 313 MW of power generation capacity (169 MW hydropower; 144 MW wind), 2) power-related construction, 3) residential and industrial real estate, and 4) nickel minerals. In our November Update report, we reiterated our BUY rating for PC1 and raised our target price (TP) by 8.0% to VND31,200/share. This is due to a 4.5% increase in our aggregate 2024-2028F NPAT-MI forecast (respective changes of +10%/0%/+7%/+5%/+3%) and the positive impact of rolling our TP forward to end-2025. Our higher aggregate earnings forecast is driven by our 24% higher aggregate NPAT-MI projection for the nickel mining business, fueled by our 55% higher sales volume across the forecast period. This outweighs our 4.9% lower aggregate NPAT-MI from power construction (mainly due to respective 30%/28% lower earnings projections for 2024/25F). We project 2025F NPAT-MI to rise 67% YoY, driven by (1) 26% YoY revenue growth in power construction, with a 2.8x expansion in 500kV line length for 2026-2030 vs 2021-2025 and renewed investment in renewables following

Phosphorus expanding, new contribution from ethanol We downgrade our rating for DGC from BUY to OUTPERFORM despite raising our target price (TP) by 27% as we roll our TP horizon from end-2024 to mid-2025, raise our aggregate 2024-26F earnings forecast by 10%, and raise our target EV/EBITDA multiple. DGC’s share price has rallied 33% over the past three months. We cut our 2024F core EBITDA by 11% due to the lower-than-expected yellow phosphorus (P4) price. However, we raise our respective 2025F/2026F core EBITDA by 15%/27%, resulting from the addition of the new ethanol factory and increased phosphorus revenue projections. We also increase our target EV/EBITDA from 7.0x to 8.5x due to (1) our 49% core EBITDA growth forecast for 2025 and (2) DGC’s expanded phosphate rock reserve that could extend the current 80%+ phosphorus ROIC into the next seven years. Our new TP puts DGC’s respective 2024F/25F P/E at 14.5x/9.6x, higher than the five-year average of DGC’s TTM P/E of 9.1x. DGC announced preliminary Q1 2024 NPAT of VND700bn (-15% YoY) due to maintenance and weak P4 prices. However, DGC has resumed operating at full capacity utilization since March. We forecast 2025 NPAT growth of 51% YoY to VND5.6tn (USD223mn), driven by (1) 9% growth in each of phosphorus sales volume and ASP, (2) VND200bn from the new ethanol factory, (3) VND100bn from the chlor-alkali project, and (4) VND329bn from a one-off property project. Progressive recovery in IPC sales volume and selling prices. We forecast IPC sales volume (in phosphorus content) to grow 17% YoY to 66,888 tonnes in 2024. DGC acquired a factory in early 2023, increasing its phosphorus capacity from 60k to 70k tonnes/year. ASP should rise because East Asia currently buys P4 at USD4,200/tonne compared to Indian customers’ USD3,700/tonne. However, this translates to a 3% YoY compression in 2024F IPC ASP. DGC acquires ethanol factory at attractive valuation. DGC recently won Agribank’s auction for Dai Viet Ltd's factory in Dak Nong Province for USD10mn. The factory converts tapioca from farmers into 96% ethanol for F&B and medical use. We note that demand for the 99% ethanol segment fell due to a 65% drop in Vietnam's E5 gasoline consumption from 2018 to 2023. DGC's factory is one of seven ethanol plants in Vietnam, representing 10% of total capacity. Per DGC, contrary to media reports, the factory was performing well before DGC's acquisition, and DGC plans to operate it in Q3 2024. We forecast 2024F and 2025F revenue of VND750bn and VND1.5tn, respectively. 2025F revenue includes 64,000 cbm of ethanol (full capacity) sold at VND20mn/cbm and VND200bn of byproducts. We increase our assumption of phosphate rock cost savings by VND4tn (USD160mn) for 2025-2030. DGC plans to spend VND10bn in 2024 to at least increase its total reserve by 50%, via doubling its mining site No. 25. It plans to acquire more mines for a 15-year self-supply of rock, compared to its current reserve lasting five years. The company will also pursue exploration and participation in State auctions for new mines. A play on structural transition to imported LNG We nudge up our target price by 0.3% as we raise 2020F earnings by 6.2% thanks to higher gas prices for industrial parks, while keeping 2021-2030F earnings nearly unchanged. However, we upgrade our rating from OUTPERFORM to BUY as GAS’s share price has declined ~10% in the past three months. We forecast 2021F NPAT-MI to recover by 21.9% YoY, mainly due to a recovery in gas volume (thanks to the contribution from the Sao Vang-Dai Nguyet gas field that is expected to come online in mid-2021) and a ~30% recovery in fuel oil (FO) price. We estimate an EPS CAGR of 7.8% in 2019-2030F, driven by more profit coming from the transportation segment (driven by LNG) that outweighs declining profits from the gas trading segment when cheap gas fields run out of reserves. We project profit from the transportation segment to double and contribute ~70% to GAS’s net profit by 2025F vs ~40% in 2019. GAS has strong financial capacity with USD1.4bn of cash on hand and net gearing of -62.3%. GAS’s valuation looks attractive at a 2021F P/E of 15.0x — 21.5% lower than its regional peer median. Upside risk: faster-than-expected gas contribution from the Sao Vang-Dai Nguyet project. Downside risk: lower-than-expected oil prices. We maintain our average Brent and FO price at USD35/bbl and USD228/ton for 2020F, before recovering to USD60/bbl and USD299/ton from 2021F, respectively. The average Brent oil price in H1 dropped by 35.3% YoY to USD42/bbl and the average FO price declined by 42.7% YoY to USD228/ton. H1 average FO price was in line with our forecast. We raise gas price assumption for industrial parks following H1 2020 results. According to PGD (GAS’s subsidiary that distributes gas for industrial park), the average gas selling price for industrial parks in H1 was USD8.7/MMBTU, which was 32% higher than our previous forecast of USD6.6/MMBTU for 2020F. For H2 2020, we continue to expect that gas prices for industrial parks will follow the market price mechanism (85% of medium fuel oil price, FO) and decline to USD5.3/MMBTU. As a result, we raise our 2020F average gas price assumption for industrial parks by 6% to USD7.0/MMBTU, which is the underlying reason for lifting 2020F NPAT-MI by 6.2%. The construction progress of the Thi Vai LNG terminal is currently ahead of schedule; GAS is preparing for capacity expansion to 3-6MMTPA. We believe this terminal could come online in late 2022. Meanwhile, GAS in talks with OIL about acquiring the latter’s stake in Petec in order for GAS to have more land and increase Thi Vai LNG terminal’s capacity from three to six million tons per annum (MMTPA) — ~70% of GAS’s current annual volume — in the longer term. COVID-19 hurts electronic goods but boosts grocery sales We attended MWG’s investor calls on August 20, 2021. Management discussed MWG’s business performance and outlook amid the current COVID-19 situation in Vietnam as well as MWG’s plans to cope with the resulting challenges. Given that the current COVID-19 situation is significantly more severe than we anticipated — which is undermining the operations of TGDD and DMX stores — we see potential downside risk to our current forecasts, pending a fuller review. Management holds a conservative view regarding consumer demand for mobile phones and consumer electronics in 2021 and 2022. However, if the current social distancing restrictions ease in Q4 2021, management believe TGDD and DMX can still achieve its 2021 guidance of combined revenue of ~VND94tn (USD4bn; +7% YoY). On the other hand, as COVID-19 disruptions to traditional retail channels (e.g., wet markets and street shops) are boosting traffic to BHX stores, management expects BHX to achieve EBITDA breakeven in full-year 2021, which is better than its previous target of reaching an EBITDA breakeven point by the end of 2021. 1. July and 7M 2021 results 1.1. Total revenue and TGDD/DMX Total revenue increased 10% YoY to VND9.5tn (USD413mn) in July 2021, of which TGDD and DMX’s aggregate revenue plunged 23% YoY as COVID-19 escalated in Vietnam. Per management, nearly 2,000 TGDD and DMX stores are affected by the social distancing restrictions (i.e., stores either undergoing temporary closure or restrictions in terms of the number of people allowed inside) and these stores typically contribute more than 80% to TGDD and DMX’s total revenue. Given even tighter social distancing restrictions in August 2021, management expects this to be another challenging month for TGDD and DMX. For 7M 2021, total revenue advanced 12% YoY, of which TGDD and DMX’s aggregate revenue stepped up 3% YoY mainly thanks to the continued rollout of super-mini-DMX stores (120-150 sqm/store), whose store count increased by 287 in 7M 2021 to 589. Super-mini-DMX contributed revenue of VND3.5tn (USD152mn) in 7M 2021, which was equivalent to 6% of TGDD and DMX’s combined revenue. 1.2. BHX BHX’s revenue reached VND17.6tn (USD764mn; +57% YoY) in 7M 2021 primarily on the back of store expansion and — to a smaller extent — SSSG (2% in H1 2021). Notably, BHX’s revenue reached VND4.2tn (USD182mn; +133% YoY) in July 2021 with sales/store jumping from more than VND1.4bn (USD61,000) in June 2021 to more than VND2.1bn (USD91,000) in July 2021. As we mentioned above, we attribute this improvement in BHX’s sales/store mainly to COVID-19 disruptions to traditional retail channels in southern Vietnam — BHX’s stronghold area. On the other hand, BHX’s GPM slid slightly to under 26% in July 2021 (vs more than 26% in June 2021) due to elevated supply-chain costs. Management also provided more colors on BHX’s performance in Q2 and H1 2021: Fresh and frozen products, packaged F&B and other FMCG products (e.g., home care and personal care) accounted for 45%, 40% and 15% of BHX’s revenue in H1 2021, respectively. In Q2 2021, monthly revenue/store averaged more than VND1.3bn (USD57,000) with SSSG of 15%, which increased from more than VND1.1bn (USD48,000) and -10% in Q1 2021, respectively. These improvements helped BHX to achieve EBITDA breakeven at the company level in Q2 2021 vs an EBITDA margin of -5% in Q1 2021. The “500-sqm” format reached a total store count of 435 as of June 2021 (of which a third was located in HCMC) and contributed 36% to BHX’s total sales. Context:
Earnings slightly beat on lower SG&A expenses SAB released Q4 2020 results. Revenue declined 19% YoY but NPAT-MI jumped 45% YoY on the back of (1) GPM expansion, (2) lower operating expenses and (3) lack of one-off provision expenses such as those related to a minority-stake investment that were recognized in Q4 2019. For full-year 2020, revenue and NPAT-MI dipped 26% YoY and 7% YoY, respectively. Revenue missed our forecast by 4%, which we think could be attributable to (1) a later Tet (Lunar New Year) in 2021 (February 12) vs 2020 (January 25) that might have affected the timing of distribution channel stocking of SAB and (2) the record flooding in the central region in Q4 2020 that hurt this region’s beer consumption. Nonetheless, NPAT-MI surpassed our forecast by 2% as SG&A expenses trailed our projections. Beer GPM remained robust as it expanded by 4.5 ppts YoY and 50 bps QoQ to 35.0% in Q4 2020. We attribute such improvements to favorable input material prices (both raw materials and packaging) and transportation costs in addition to improvements in operational efficiency. SG&A/beer revenue % climbed to 14.3% in 2020 from 12.4% in 2019, which was primarily driven by a 6% rise in advertising and promotion (A&P) expenses amid heightening competition. In Q4 2020, A&P expenses shrank 20% YoY (flat QoQ); however, we think this YoY decline could be due to the timing of SAB’s marketing campaigns as we believe the intensity of competition is not easing. EBIT as % of beer revenue broadened by 9.4 ppts YoY and 1.6 ppts QoQ to a multiyear high of 22.8% in Q4 2020. Overall, we see non-material changes to our current forecasts, pending a fuller review. A summary of Q4 2020 and full-year 2020 financials is presented on the next page SAB’s 2020 results Source: SAB, VCSC

Context:
Convertible pref issue to help control leverage We cut our target price (TP) for GEG by 35% to VND15,100/share but upgrade our rating from MARKET PERFORM to OUTPERFORM as GEG’s share price has slumped 42% over the past three months. GEG will be the earliest beneficiary under our coverage when the renewable power sector gets a new pricing mechanism. We also believe GEG’s P/B of 0.9x is attractive. Our lower TP is due to a 21% cut in our aggregate 2022-2026F NPAT forecast and 100-bp and 200-bp increases in our equity risk premium and cost of debt assumptions, respectively. We forecast 2023F core NPAT-MI to rise 21% YoY due to higher profit from GEG’s solar portfolio and four existing wind farms despite a potential loss from the new Tan Phu Dong 1 wind farm (100 MW), which we expect to come online in early 2023. We forecast an 18% EPS CAGR in 2021-2026F, which is driven by doubling wind power capacity from two additional farms (130 MW) coming into operation after 2022, the stable operation of five solar farms and 12 hydropower plants, and declining interest expenses. Upside catalysts: Further gain from wind power divestment; earlier-than-expected operation of Duc Hue 2 solar farm (see page 5). Downside risks: Lower-than-expected loan disbursement in 2023 that fails due payables. We expect GEG to issue 64 million convertible preferred shares instead of a rights issue. In October 2022, GEG announced BOD resolutions on a convertible preferred stock private placement of 64.2 million shares for Deutsche Investitions- und Entwicklungsgesellschaft (DEG) in H1 2023 to invest in the Duc Hue 2 solar farm and VPL2 wind farm with an offering price of VND10,000/share. The exercise right is at least 24 months after issuance date, and the conversion price is negotiable, but not lower than BVPS at the issuance date. We estimate a maximum conversion ratio of 0.9 (see page 5). In our view, this six-year preferred stock issuance with a dividend of 6% par value p.a. will support GEG to manage its leverage ratio and partly ease interest expense pressure from its capacity expansion. We factor this preferred share issue into our model in 2023 and remove GEG’s previous plan to issue 30 million common shares at VND14,000/share. GEG divested its 45% stake in Tan Phu Dong 1 & 2 wind farms but maintained its 55% controlling stake. In Q3 2022, GEG completed its divestment of No. 1 New Renewable Energy JSC, which fully owns Tan Phu Dong 1 & 2. GEG recorded a gain of VND166bn under financial income in Q3 2022 and received VND864bn in cash. Per GEG, this amount is not reflected in the full transaction value; the company can recognize the remaining divestment gain in 2023. We expect the Tan Phu Dong 1 wind power project to come online by early 2023. As of end-Q3 2022, GEG had disbursed VND3.4tn for this project (~75% its total investment capital). 9M 2022 Recap: Divestment gain outweighs increased interest expenses Figure 1: GEG’s 9M 2022 results Source: GEG, VCSC 2022F: NPAT bolstered by divestment gain Figure 2: VCSC’s 2022 forecast for GEG Source: GEG, VCSC 2023F: Rising interest expenses to dampen earnings Figure 3: VCSC’s 2023 forecast for GEG Source: GEG, VCSC Divestment gain On June 3, 2022, GEG’s BOD approved the divestment of No. 1 New Renewable Energy JSC. Context:
Tiềm năng tăng trưởng nhờ vị thế tiền mặt lớn Chúng tôi công bố báo cáo lần đầu cho CTCP Dịch vụ Hàng hóa Sài Gòn (SCS) với khuyến nghị KHẢ QUAN, tương ứng với tổng mức sinh lời 10,0% đã bao gồm lợi suất cổ tức 5,5%. Tăng trưởng lợi nhuận sẽ được dẫn dắt sẽ được bởi vị thế thị trường mạnh mẽ với rào cản gia nhập ngành cao và nguồn cung hạn chế. SCS là công ty duy nhất có công suất còn trống để được hưởng lợi từ tăng trưởng sản lượng hàng hóa hàng không ở miền Nam. Thành lập năm 2010, SCS đang bước vào giai đoạn tăng trưởng, phần lớn tăng trưởng sản lượng hàng hóa bổ sung dự kiến phần lớn sẽ được chuyển hóa thành lợi nhuận. Chúng tôi dự báo tốc độ tăng trưởng kép (CAGR) của doanh thu và LNST của SCS trong giai đoạn 2016-2021 lần lượt là 17,5% và 24,7%, chủ yếu được dẫn dắt bởi tăng trưởng cao của lượng hàng hóa thông qua cảng. P/E trượt của SCS là 17,5 lần, hợp lý với tăng trưởng EPS mạnh mẽ 2017 là 30,7%. Các rủi ro chính bao gồm: (1) Hạn chế công suất của sân bay Tân Sơn Nhất, (2) lưu lượng hàng hóa biến động và (3) các thương vụ M&A tiềm năng do công ty thực hiện. Ngành vận chuyển hàng không ở Việt Nam dự kiến sẽ tăng trưởng với CAGR 15,3% trong 15 năm tới tính theo số tấn hàng hóa, theo Cục Hàng không Dân dụng Việt Nam (CAAV). Tăng trưởng sẽ được củng cố bởi dòng thương mại, đầu tư đáng kể vào cơ sở hạ tầng hàng không, thu nhập thặng dự gia tăng và nhu cầu cao cho vận chuyển hàng không. Tăng trưởng lượng hàng hóa xử lý tại sân bay Tân Sơn Nhất sẽ giúp SCS hưởng lợi khi đối thủ cạnh tranh duy nhất đã chạy tối đa công suất. Công ty TNHH Dịch vụ Hàng hóa Tân Sơn Nhất (TCS), đối thủ cạnh tranh của SCS và là công ty con của Tổng CT Hàng không Quốc gia Việt Nam (Vietnam Airlines), hiện đang hoạt động tối đa công suất và không còn quỹ đất trống để mở rộng. Trong khi đó, SCS hiện đang hoạt động với 70% công suất của công suất giai đoạn 1 (200.000 tấn hàng hóa/năm) và có thể dễ dàng tăng công suất lên mức tối đa là 350.000 tấn/năm. Yêu cầu đầu tư vốn thấp để tận dụng tăng trưởng thị trường. Do nhà ga hiện tại được xây với công suất của giai đoạn 2 và máy mọc hiện tại thì đủ cho giai đoạn 1 nên SCS chỉ cần đầu tư 7-10 triệu USD trong 7 năm tới vào máy móc mới để đạt công suất tối đa. Tắc nghẽn giao thông hàng không vẫn là rủi ro của công ty. Khi sân bay Tân Sơn Nhất đang đối mặt với tình trạng vượt công suất nghiêm trọng khi hiệu suất hoạt động đang ở mức 120% và hiện vẫn chưa có kế hoạch mở rộng cụ thể, tình trạng tắc nghẽn có thể giới hạng tăng trưởng lượng hàng hóa xử lý của công ty. Dòng tiền mặt tạo ra lớn và bảng cân đối kế toán mạnh mẽ có thể sẽ dẫn đến các hoạt động M&A. Dòng tiền HĐKD ổn định và mạnh mẽ của SCS, nhờ sự hỗ trợ tăng trưởng hàng hóa xử lý lớn, khiến tỷ lệ

Context:
Maintenance and possible tariff hike signal gloomy 2019 DPM’s AGM was held on April 17, 2019. Shareholders raised many questions regarding its 80-day maintenance period, ultra-conservative 2019 guidance, the State’s divestment progress and its cash dividend payment. Management again set ultra-low 2019 guidance with revenue of VND8.6tn (USD371mn, -7%YoY) and NPAT of VND170bn (USD7mn, -76.1%YoY). This guidance is based on 1) urea and NH3 plants conducting maintenance for 80 days and 2) a conservative gas transportation tariff of USD1.4/MMBTU, vs a USD1/MMBTU in the current gas contract. Following these developments, we foresee a ~30% downside risk to our 2019 earnings forecast due to maintenance, another possible 15%-20% lower in 2019-2023F earnings forecast due to potential higher gas transportation tariff, therefore, 20%-25% downside risk to our current target price accordingly. Regarding the State’s divestment progress, PetroVietnam’s (PVN) representative said that PVN will accelerate its divestment plan to reduce its stake to 51% before end-2020. In addition, the representative stated that a divestment plan to 36% is currently under consideration. The merger proposal between DPM and its sister company DCM was previously considered as one of the options in the State’s divestment plan. Still, according to PVN’s representative, PVN is likely to separately reduce stake in DPM and DCM to 51%, rather than undergoing divestment after the merger. Lower urea and NPK volume guidance due to 80 days of maintenance. This maintenance is scheduled to last from February 19, 2019 to May 10, 2019, which longer than DPM’s normal bi-annual maintenance of 22-26 days. This longer maintenance period is due to DPM combining its periodic maintenance for its urea plant as well as unexpected technical issues related to upgrading the heat exchange reformer (HTER) of the NH3 expansion plant. Hence, DPM targets for a urea sales volume of 680,000 tons (85% utilization rate) and NPK sale volume of 155,000 tons (62% utilization rate) for 2019. These volume targets are 16.0% and 22.5% lower than our respective assumptions, implying downside risk to our forecasts. Possible hike in input gas transportation tariff. The low profit guidance is also derived from an input gas transportation tariff of USD1.4/MMBTU vs a USD1/MMBTU in the current contract. While negotiation about this tariff between DPM and PVN is still ongoing between DPM and PVN, DPM’s incorporating a tariff assumption of USD1.4/MMBTU to its 2019 guidance signals that there is a high possibility of tariff hike. Therefore, we foresee a possible higher gas transportation tariff and higher cost for DPM in the coming years. No new information regarding VAT law amendment. The VAT revision would reclassify fertilizers as eligible for input VAT deduction. This amendment needs to be submitted to National Assembly (NA) meeting for approval. However, we have not seen the VAT topic in the agenda of upcoming the May meeting, implying unlikely approval this time. If we incorporate VAT changes into our model, it could lift the earnings forecast by 20% and our target price by 10%-15% vs current forecasts. Management approved cash dividend of VND1,000/share (yield 5.5%) for 2018 and 2019. DPM advanced a 2018 interim cash dividend of VND500/share in February 2019 and will pay the remaining VND500/share in Q2 2019. Meanwhile, DPM is guiding for a 2019 cash dividend of VND1,000/share based on its conservative 2019 guidance. We previously expected a 2019 cash dividend of VND1,500/share for 2019 (yield 8.3%) thanks to ample cash on hand and an earnings recovery outlook for 2019. However, the potential gas transportation tariff hike could affect the earnings outlook and cash dividend payment, which also implies downside risk to our 2019 cash dividend forecast. New BOD member. Mr.

For 2018, QNS once again sets a low NPAT target of only VND194bn (USD8.5mn) vs 2017A NPAT of VND1.03tn (USD45.2mn). QNS has a history of setting ultra-conservative guidance. Our forecast calls for 10% growth in NPAT in 2018 vs 2017 driven by soymilk, a new contribution from the biomass power plant and lower input costs in the sugar segment. Shareholders approved a bonus share issuance at a ratio of 10:2 (ten existing shares get two new shares). FY2018 dividend is set at a minimum of 15% of par value (either cash or shares). QNS is trading at an attractive 2018 PER of 10.7x and a FCF yield of >13% starting from 2019 after disbursements for sugar and biomass capex are completed. Downside to our FCF forecasts: QNS’s possible investment in a Refined Extra (RE) sugar production line, whose estimated capex equals to 75% of our 2019 FCF forecast. Recurring ultra-conservative profit targets are not indicative of actual results. Per management, QNS’s lowball profit guidance every year results from its long-established internal compensation system, i.e., how employees get paid, which takes into account how the company and its divisions exceed their assigned targets. Given this, we believe that the company should provide another “external” profit guidance to avoid confusion among investors. Figure 1 below shows QNS’s target and actual NPAT between 2015 and 2017. QNS’s sugarcane volume surged on the back of favorable weather and the fact that the current low sugar price environment has wiped out several smaller competitors from the market. Moreover, we note that Q1 2017 was a low base in terms of both sugar volume and margins as severe flooding at the beginning of 2017 in Gia Lai province (QNS’s major plantation area) dampened sugarcane yield and CCS (commercial cane sugar). QNS plans to launch two to four new soymilk products during September-December 2018. In these products, QNS will add different nutritional ingredients to soymilk to cater to a wider range of consumers. These launches will be supported by a new in-house marketing team, led by an ex-Unilever employee. This is a key development to watch in 2018 for QNS given the importance of product innovation in accelerating the company’s soymilk growth while innovation has not been an area of strength for the company in the past four years. Management still has high ambition for sugar, which raises questions about capital allocation. QNS aims to account for one-third of Vietnam’s total sugar consumption by 2020, compared to ~12% currently. To reach this goal, the company is considering an investment in a RE sugar production line, which will enable it to establish a retail sugar brand. Currently, QNS is only producing Refined Standard (RS) sugar and wholesaling it to traders. The maximum budget for this RE sugar project is set at USD52mn for a total capacity of 1,000 tonnes of sugar per day (using raw sugar as input). The company has recently begun to welcome bids from international contractors with construction likely to take 12-15 months. As such, if things go as planned, this RE sugar processing plant will likely come online in H2 2019. Efficient genco ready to pay dividends EVNGENCO 2’s IPO will take place on February 8 with 580 million shares (48.9% stake) offered at an IPO staring price of VND24,520/share. GENCO2 is the fifth largest power generator in Vietnam and had 7% market share (in term of output) as of 2019. It expects to convert into a joint stock company (JSC) and list on UPCoM in April with the ticker GE2. We estimate a fair value at end-2021 of VND27,700/share, implying a prospective TSR of 17.5% (including a 4.5% dividend yield). We believe GE2 offers attractive exposure to expected robust electricity consumption growth in Vietnam that will be led by further industrialization. We expect adjusted NPAT-MI to grow 5.1% YoY from a high base in 2020F mainly due to significantly higher water levels across GE2’s hydropower reservoirs in early 2021 vs early 2020. We project that GE2 will deliver flat adjusted EPS, average ROE of 12% (40% higher than peers) and a cash dividend CAGR of 6% (thanks to strong financial capacity) in 2020-2025F. Valuations at the IPO price are compelling at a 2021F EV/EBITDA of 6.6x (33% cheaper vs the regional peer four-year average multiple) and P/E of 12-13x (10% cheaper vs the regional peer four-year average), based on our forecast. GE2 also beats peers on ROE and dividend yield. Upsides to our forecast: Potential contribution from new solar capacity; O Mon 1 upgrade. Downside risks: Fluctuating rainfall; booking of unrealized FX loss. Hydropower plants (~50% of NPAT-MI) have lowest production cost in the system. As a result, it is easy for GE2’s hydropower portfolio to compete in the competitive generation market (CGM). The portfolio includes high-quality and young hydropower plants (~10 years old vs a 40-year lifetime) with reasonable capex and low financing rates. In addition, most of GE2’s hydropower plants are in central Vietnam, which experiences the highest amount of rainfall in the country. GE2’s coal-fired portfolio (~50% of NPAT-MI) includes Pha Lai Thermal Power (HSX: PPC) and Hai Phong Thermal Power (UPCoM: HND). We see a bright outlook for GE2’s coal-fired portfolio on the back of its ideal location and strong operational efficiency based on the companies’ long histories of operation. We forecast PPC to pay a 10%-12% dividend yield and HND to pay a sustainable 6%-8% dividend yield. GE2’s gas-fired plant (O Mon 1) is poised to ramp up its utilization rate from 2024. O Mon 1 is designed to use natural gas from the Block B gas field; however, this gas field is not currently operational and has been underutilized in the past. Recent PetroVietnam guidance suggests that Block B will become operational in 2024; however, we have not incorporated this potential upgrade project in our forecast. It is noteworthy that O Mon 1 has been able secure enough revenue to pay for principal and interest expenses thus far. Context:
Power and M&E segments drag on earnings REE recorded lackluster quarterly results with NPAT-MI sliding 27% YoY to reach VND256bn (USD11mn) in Q1 2020. Overall, we see some downside risk to our 2020F earnings forecast for REE as Q1 2020 NPAT-MI completed only 16% of our full-year forecast, which was mainly due to the underperformance of the power and mechanical & engineering (M&E) segments. We note that according to a forecast from the Vietnam Institute of Meteorology, Hydrology and Climate Change, there could be a material recovery of rainfall in H2 2020 compared to 2019. Therefore, we expect REE’s hydropower plant’s earnings to be materially better in the second half of the year. Power and water NPAT-MI plummeted 46% YoY in Q1 2020 as declining performance of power plants outweighed strong growth in water utility companies. In Q1 2020, we observed that most of REE’s major power plants recorded a sizable retreat in earnings, including Pha Lai Thermal Power (PPC; Q1 2020 NPAT down 44% YoY), Vinh Son Hydropower (VSH; Q1 2020 NPAT down 100% YoY) and Thac Mo Hydropower (TMP; Q1 2020 NPAT down 29% YoY), which fell short of our power segment forecast. We attribute the underperformance of REE’s overall hydropower portfolio to a continued drought in Q1 2020 that was due to a significant decline in rainfall. On the other hand, PPC’s decline in earnings was mainly attributable to higher maintenance expenses — a company-specific event (see our April 15, 2020 PPC Earnings Flash). Meanwhile, we estimate that total shared profit from REE’s associated water companies jumped 36% YoY in Q1 2020, which is slightly ahead of our water segment forecast. Net profit margins of the M&E segment halved YoY due to mounting costs on the back of COVID-19 driven project delays. While REE’s M&E revenue was flat YoY in Q1 2020, the segment’s NPM contracted from 8.1% Q1 2019 to 4.4% in Q1 2020, which is due to the early impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on project developments, especially in March 2020, in our view. Overall M&E revenue and NPAT-MI reached only 17% and 9% of our full-year forecast, respectively, implying downside risk to our M&E segment forecast — especially to our margin assumptions. Office leasing recorded a healthy 17% increase in NPAT-MI thanks to contributions of Etown 5, which commenced operation in Q2 2019. The office leasing segment performed as expected in Q1 2020 with revenue and NPAT-MI reached 22% and 24% of our respective full-year office leasing segment forecasts. The Classia project (formerly named Armena; total project includes ~180 low-rise units in Thu Duc City, HCMC) started construction at end-September 2021 when COVID-19 social distancing measures started to ease. Furthermore, we expect the company will push the construction progress for Binh Tan (a high-rise project in Binh Tan District, HCMC) and Clarita (a low-rise project in Thu Duc City, HCMC). As such, we expect these new projects will drive our 2022F residential pre-sales value forecast for KDH to reach VND4.2tn (USD182.6mn). We continue to forecast the two low-rise projects — Classia and Clarita — and the expected IP land sales at the Le Minh Xuan Industrial Zone expansion project (110 ha in HCMC) will support our 2022F NPAT-MI forecast of VND1.6tn (USD71.5mn; +40% vs 2021F). KDH’s share price has increased 22% over the past three months on the back of on-track handovers in Q3 2021 and expectations for new project launches. We view KDH as fully valued with a 2021/2022F P/E of 26.7x/19.6x and 2021/2022F P/B of 2.9x/2.5x (based on our forecasts) despite our strong NPAT-MI growth forecast of 40% YoY for 2022F. Upside catalyst: Higher-than-expected selling prices at newly launched projects. Downside risk: Slower-than-expected launches for large-scale projects like the Tan Tao project. We expect strong pre-sales for 2022F. We maintain our view that housing products from experienced developers with strong brand equity — such as KDH with its dominance in the HCMC landed housing segment — will continue attract interest from homebuyers. Therefore, we continue to expect strong pre-sales for KDH’s new residential projects that will launch in 2022 — Classia, Clarita and Binh Tan. We forecast 2022F residential pre-sales at VND4.2tn (USD182.6mn) vs 2021F’s low base of VND1.0tn (USD45.4mn; mostly from the remaining units at Lovera Vista and Verosa Park). We expect new project launches to drive strong earnings growth in 2022F. Per management, the company will hold a soft launch event for Classia pre-sales by end-2021. In addition, KDH expects to obtain necessary legal approvals for the Clarita, Binh Tan and Le Minh Xuan Industrial Zone expansion projects in 2022. We forecast the two low-rise projects Classia and Clarita will contribute ~60% of our 2022F NPAT-MI forecast while the remainder will come from expected IP land sales in 2022 at the Le Minh Xuan Industrial Zone expansion project. 9M 2021 Recap: Safira handovers complete; Lovera Vista handovers recognized in Q2-Q3 earnings Figure 1: 9M 2021 results Source: Company’s Q3 2021 financial statements, VCSC forecast 2021 Outlook: Ongoing handovers at Lovera Vista, Verosa Park to support Q4 earnings Figure 2: 2021 forecast Source: Company’s financial statements, VCSC forecast 2022 Outlook: New low-rise projects, IP land sales to drive earnings growth Figure 3: 2022 forecast Source: Company’s financial statements, VCSC forecast Figure 4: KDH’s key projects located in eastern and southwestern HCMC Source: KDH, VCSC compilation. Note: (*) Le Minh Xuan Industrial Park expansion project. Figure 5: Forecast for construction and handover pipeline of major residential projects Source: VCSC projection. Note: (*) VCSC assumptions; 11A, Phong Phu

We reaffirm our **MARKET PERFORM** rating on KDH, adjusting the price targe[5D[K target upwards by 11% to **VND45,000 per share [S1]**. This valuation incor[5D[K incorporates a longer horizon extending to the end of 2022 alongside optimi[6D[K optimistic assumptions regarding residential selling prices for upcoming de[2D[K developments. The stock has appreciated **22% in the last three months [S9][4D[K [S9]**, a move driven by strong handover volumes in the third quarter. Recent earnings performance validates our expectations, with net profit att[3D[K attributable to the majority interest (NPAT-MI) for the first nine months r[1D[K reaching **VND788bn [S2]**, a marginal increase of 2% year-over-year. We ma[2D[K maintain our 2021 full-year guidance, projecting **NPAT-MI to remain flat a[1D[K at VND1.2tn [S3]**. Growth is expected to accelerate in 2022, where we anti[4D[K anticipate NPAT-MI to grow **40% year-over-year to VND1.6tn [S4]**. This ex[2D[K expansion is underpinned by a significant increase in residential pre-sales[9D[K pre-sales volumes, which are forecast to reach **VND4.2tn**, compared to th[2D[K the prior year's baseline of **VND1.0tn [S5]**. The 2022 earnings outlook relies heavily on specific project milestones. Lo[2D[K Low-rise initiatives, specifically **Classia and Clarita**, are expected to[2D[K to contribute approximately **60% of the 2022 NPAT-MI forecast [S6]**. The [K remaining earnings will be supported by **IP land sales from the Le Minh Xu[2D[K Xuan Industrial Zone expansion [S7]**. While the outlook remains robust, the current valuation already incorporate[11D[K incorporates this growth, presenting a **2022 P/E of 19.6x [S8]**. Key upsi[4D[K upside drivers involve **premium pricing on new launches**, while the prima[5D[K primary downside risk remains the potential for delayed execution on large-[6D[K large-scale ventures like the **Tan Tao project [S10]**. Despite this, the [K company retains a competitive advantage in the **high-rise and landed segme[5D[K segments within HCMC**, ensuring continued buyer interest in their product [K portfolio.

Context:
of the best ways to capitalize on Vietnam’s ballooning middle-affluent class, in our view. PNJ is the dominant leader of Vietnam’s fashion gold jewelry market (at least 60% market share, per our estimate). Rising disposable income is fostering indulgent consumption in Vietnam that should propel rapid growth for fashion gold jewelry, in our opinion. PNJ’s leadership has been solidified after smaller competitors struggled financially due to COVID-19. Investments in technology and second factory to cement PNJ’s pole position. A new ERP system and data analytics capabilities will help PNJ to deepen its understanding of customer behavior and product performance while also strengthening its inventory management and online sales execution. In addition, PNJ is upgrading its second factory to facilitate its medium-term growth targets. We forecast a retail revenue CAGR of 20% during 2020-2023F, including 25% growth in 2021, which is premised on projected double-digit SSSG and 35 new gold stores (net of store closures) per annum. We expect a further boost to the bottom line from improvements in product mix and operational efficiency. The fourth wave of COVID-19 in Vietnam to temporarily disrupt PNJ’s store operations — especially in its mainstay market of HCMC. That said, as proven in 2020, PNJ captures an affluent customer base whose income and spending power are resilient against the impact from COVID-19. We find PNJ’s three-year PEG of 0.9 (based on our forecasts) attractive. Potential upside catalysts: Successful entry into adjacent categories in the female fashion and beauty space. Risks to our positive view: Prolonged COVID-19 disruptions that would undermine consumer demand as well as store operations; slower-than-expected store expansion. Company Overview: PNJ is the leading jewelry producer and retailer in Vietnam with an extensive store network of 343 jewelry stores and 69 watch point-of-sales as of May 2021. We expect jewelry retailing will continue to be PNJ’s future growth driver. QNS – Sugar business a distraction from dominant soymilk franchise 35 QNS is Vietnam’s dominant branded soymilk player with an 85% market share in 2020. Its leading position is premised on its first-mover advantage, trusted brands and extensive distribution. In our view, QNS’s current valuation discount vs pure-play F&B peers is warranted given its ineffective capital allocation that leans toward the volatile, commodity-based sugar business. We project NPAT-MI will deliver an 11% CAGR during 2020-2023F, which is primarily backed by a potential recovery in soymilk demand from COVID-19 disruptions (we project a 7% soymilk revenue CAGR for QNS during 2020-2023F) as well as a strengthening in sugarcane yields and sugar selling prices that benefit the sugar business. For 2021, we forecast a 25% surge in EPS mostly thanks to an elevated ASP in the sugar segment. In June 2021, the Government announced official antidumping tariffs (47% of import prices) on sugar imported from Thailand. This official announcement replaced temporary antidumping tariffs that were imposed in February 2021. Potential upside catalysts: Corporate events that provide price discovery for QNS’s soymilk business, which we believe could be valued higher by the market if viewed separately; stronger-than-expected sugar prices that could boost investor sentiment apart from lifting QNS’s profits. Downside risks: Rising competition in soymilk; sugar price fluctuations; unfavorable weather that hurts sugarcane yields. Company Overview Quang Ngai Sugar is the leading soymilk company and one of the largest sugar producers in Vietnam. Its other businesses include beer, mineral water, confectionery and biomass power generation. SAB – Earnings to resume growth on industry recovery, new products 36 SAB is well positioned to capture Vietnam’s rising beer consumption on the back of its household brands and extensive distribution. There is also potential for margin expansion from operational

Context:
new IP establishment, in our view) to accelerate vs in 2023 due to (1) our expectation for more provincial masterplan approvals and (2) current high occupancy rates in most tier-1 industrial provinces in both northern and southern Vietnam. For the medium term, we expect that the supply in tier-1 industrial regions will remain tight as new approvals in 2020-2023 have mostly focused on tier-2 industrial areas. Our top picks are IDC, KBC, SIP, and LHG. We favor IP developers with large, cleared industrial land areas in tier-1 industrial regions given high occupancy rates and resilient FDI inflows into these regions. We like IDC, SIP, and LHG as these IP developers should be able to leverage their existing land banks to capitalize on current favorable market conditions and have healthy financial positions which should enable them to prepare for future growth phases over the long term. We like KBC as it is the chief beneficiary of the shift in high-tech manufacturing to northern Vietnam. KBC has been able to attract brand-name tenants such as LG, Foxconn, and Goertek, and also benefits from its undervalued residential land bank in industrial hubs. Figure 430: Industrial parks: Key data Figure 431: Industrial parks: Summary valuations Source: Company data, Vietcap forecast. Note: Share prices as of December 29, 2023; VGC forecasts from our energy team’s coverage of GEX; SZC’s share price and target price are adjusted for rights issue (XR January 5, 2024); GVR updated on January 3, 2024. Sector outlook Vietnam's value chain advancement and relocation by global manufacturers to support IP land demand Recovery in manufacturing and a pivot in monetary policy in 2024 to drive IP land demand. We expect aggregate IP land sales of IP stocks under our coverage to achieve 22% growth in 2024F vs strong growth of 49% in 2023F. We expect northern IPs to maintain strong with IP land sales and FDI registrations, driven by continuing demand for the high-growth electronics sector. Meanwhile, we expect IP land sales in southern regions to pick up vs in 2023 due to an expected recovery in demand in key sectors such as machinery & equipment, steel, and textiles & garments. Additionally, a pivot in monetary policy from key central banks such as the US Federal Reserve, as per current consensus forecasts, should be more supportive of capital expenditure by manufacturers relative to the aggressive tightening seen in 2022/23. In 2023, Vietnam’s new FDI registrations increased by 32.1% YoY to USD36.6bn (vs an annual average in 2012-2022 of USD27.7bn) despite a global economic slowdown. FDI registrations in 2023 to the northern and central regions increased by 50% and 49% YoY, respectively, while FDI registrations to the southern region recorded weaker growth at 8% YoY. We attribute this strong increase in the North to the ongoing shift in high-tech manufacturing from China, in addition to resilient export demand for electronic products. Meanwhile, the weaker performance in the South was mainly due to slower export demand in major sectors in the region, including machinery & equipment, textiles & garments, and iron & steel. Figure 432: Disbursed FDI, registered FDI, and quarterly average of disbursed FDI (USD mn) Source: Ministry of Planning and Investment, Vietcap Among the IP stocks that we follow, we forecast aggregate IP land sales in 2023/24/25F to increase by 49%/22%/7% YoY to 619 ha/755 ha/804 ha, respectively. The increase in 2023F is mainly driven by a huge forecast jump in IP land sales at KBC of 8.6x YoY to 162 ha. Meanwhile, for 2024F, we expect IP land sales for KBC and IDC to be sustained at 173 ha (+7% YoY)
