Context:
Industrial Manufacturers Uncertain outlook amid tariff situation June 2025 Vy Nguyen Senior Manager vy.nguyen@vietcap.com.vn +8428 3914 3588 ext.147 Han Nguyen Analyst han.nguyengoc@vietcap.com.vn +8428 3914 3588 ext.191 2 Summary Cautious outlook amid an uncertain global environment, particularly fast-changing US tariff policies Vietnam’s key export sectors - textile and wood & wooden products – have shown YoY orders improvement in 4M 2025, largely driven by restocking activity among major markets as distributors/retailers operate with healthier inventory levels. However, ASPs remained subdued as pricing pressure from downstream players aims to stimulate end-consumer demand. Textile sector: US manufacturers and brands keep a conservative outlook on customer spending amid rising inflation and interest rates. Market consensus reflects a weaker US outlook while remaining more upbeat for China. Wood & wooden products sector: US mortgage rates remained high but cooled YoY in 4M 2025, setting the stage for a gradual recovery in housing permits and, consequently, furniture imports. We expect high-single-digit growth for textiles and mid-teen growth for wooden furniture products for the full-year 2025. Other indicators suggest a gradual recovery of exports in 2025 Increasing chip sales, coupled with manufacturers’ healthy inventory levels, should boost yellow phosphorus demand. Persistently low plastic prices continue to support margins for companies with high exposure to plastic input costs. Downside risks Higher-than-expected US tariff hitting global consumption and raising Vietnam’s export costs. Vietnam: Weaker VND could lead to large FX losses for companies with unhedged exposure. Stock picks DGC: Growing yellow phosphorus demand and contributions from new projects are set to drive growth. Notably, DGC has minimal exposure to the US market, only 2% of revenue, and can easily redirect this share to the East Asia and India markets. DGC’s 2025F/26F forward EV/EBITDAs are 6.6x/5.8x, respectively, representing 27%/36% discount vs its 10Y average EV/EBITDA of 9.0x. TLG: Sustainable growth underpinned by continued export market expansion, a strategic shift toward high-margin segments (e.g., art supplies and innovative products), and tailwinds from low plastic input costs. TLG’s 2025–26F average forward P/E of 10.1x vs its 5Y average of 11x, implies a PEG of 0.84 amid a sustainable 12% EPS CAGR over 2024–2027F. 3 Industrials: Key data and summary valuations Note: Share prices as of June 5, 2025. Source: Company data, Vietcap Key data Summary valuation 4 US reciprocal tariff impacts on export companies under our coverage Vietcap’s comments: Overall, at any tariff rate, a higher US tariff adds substantial cost pressure on Vietnam’s exporters as well as US brands/customers and potentially weigh on global end-customer demand. Following that, we anticipate a challenge for Vietnam’s export sectors, particularly sectors having high US exposure such as textiles (40% exposure) and wood & wood products (50% exposure). However, based on President Trump’s emphasis that US tariff policies aim to encourage and support domestic manufacturing of advanced technology and defense equipment (e.g. military equipment, AI products, chips, etc.) not consumer goods (e.g. textiles and others), we expect a more moderate tariff approach toward Vietnam’s key export categories. The newly revised US tariff policy, which imposes a 10% increase on top of existing rates for most countries (excluding China, which faces a 30% hike), results in higher duties across the board. While this raises cost pressures for Vietnamese exporters, the significantly higher tariffs on Chinese goods could present an opportunity for Vietnam to capture additional US market share—partially offsetting the impact of elevated duties. Prior to this revision, Vietnam’s exports to the US faced tariffs ranging from 3.3% to 18.5% for textiles (HS 61 & 62), while most wood & wooden products (HS 94, 4407, 4403) were duty-free, except for plywood (HS 4412), which was subject

Context:
Ban lãnh đạo nhận thấy nhiều thách thức do COVID-19 ĐHCĐ của Tổng CTCP Khoan và dịch vụ khoan dầu khí (PVD) diễn ra ngày 18/06/2020. Ban lãnh đạo giải quyết các câu hỏi từ phía cổ đông xoay quanh triển vọng kinh doanh năm 2020, tiến độ liên quan đến hợp đồng dài hạn cho giàn khoan nước sâu (TAD) và thu hồi nợ xấu từ Tổng CT Thăm dò Khai thác Dầu khí Việt Nam (PVEP). PVD đặt mục tiêu cho năm 2020 với doanh thu đạt 201 triệu USD (+7,1% YoY) và LNST sau lợi ích CĐTS đạt 3 triệu USD (-64,5% YoY), dựa theo giá dầu ở mức 60 USD/thùng và trước khi bùng phát dịch COVID-19. Ban lãnh đạo cho biết dịch COVID-19 hiện ảnh hưởng các nhà vận hành trì hoãn quá trình chọn nhà thầu khoan dầu, dẫn đến khó khăn cho PVD trong việc tìm các công việc khoan dầu cho năm 2021. Tuy nhiên, chúng tôi kỳ vọng các khó khăn này sẽ hạ nhiệt vào cuối năm 2020 theo giả định dịch COVID-19 được kiểm soát và giá dầu cao hơn. Ban lãnh đạo cho biết LNST sau lợi ích CĐTS sơ bộ trong 6 tháng đầu năm đạt 3,2 triệu USD, giả định PVD có thể thu hồi khoản nợ xấu 2 triệu USD từ PVEP vào cuối tháng 6. LNST sau lợi ích CĐTS sơ bộ 6 tháng đầu năm 2020 hoàn thành 47% dự báo cả năm của chúng tôi và nhìn chung phù hợp với kỳ vọng của chúng tôi. PVD đặt mục tiêu vốn XDCB năm 2020 là 39 triệu USD, trong đó 35 triệu USD cho việc đầu tư Bộ thiết bị khoan dầu (DES) cho hợp đồng TAD 10 năm tại Brunei, bắt đầu từ quý 3/2021. Cổ đông thông qua kế hoạch tức cổ phiếu 10% cho năm 2019, dự kiến thực hiện trong 6 tháng cuối năm 2020. PVD đặt mục tiêu cho năm 2020, bao gồm doanh thu đạt 201 triệu USD (+7,1% YoY) và LNST sau lợi ích CĐTS đạt 3 triệu USD (-64,5% YoY), dựa theo kịch giá dầu đạt 60 USD/thùng). Tuy nhiên, mục tiêu LNST sau lợi ích CĐTS năm 2020 tương ứng 42% dự báo cả năm của chúng tôi. Chúng tôi lưu ý rằng PVD thường có quan điểm thận trọng trong việc lên kế hoạch (cụ thể, LNST sau lợi ích CĐTS thực tế đạt trung bình khoảng 8,4 triệu USD so với mục tiêu không lỗ trong giai đoạn 2018-2019). Các nhà vận hành ngành dầu & khí trong khu vực đã thay đổi kế hoạch khoan dầu do dịch COVID-19, dẫn đến tác động tiêu cực lên hiệu suất hoạt động của PVD. Vào đầu năm 2020, PVD kỳ vọng 4 giàn khoan tự nâng (JU) có việc làm tại Malaysia với các hợp đồng ký kết có giá thuê ngày trung bình khoảng 65.000 USD (+10% YoY). Tuy nhiên, do dịch COVID-19, các nhà vận hành ngành dầu & khí Malaysia đã đàm phán với PVD nhằm hoãn 1 lượng công việc khoan dầu trong năm 2020 sang năm 2021 – dù không có áp lực đối với giá thuê ngày. Do đó, giàn JU PVD II hiện đang tìm thêm các công việc khác cho 6 tháng cuối năm 2020 trong khi PVD III sẽ bắt đầu quá trình khoan tại Cambodia trong tháng 8 và tháng 9. PVD I và VI vẫn có công việc đến cuối năm 2020. Các diễn biến này phù hợp với dự báo

Context:
Solid Q4 2022 results — in line with expectations BWE announced solid Q4 2022 results with revenue of VND1.0tn (USD44mn; +3% YoY) and reported NPAT-MI of VND173bn (USD7mn; -29% YoY). We attribute the decline in NPAT to a jump in financial expenses and depreciation expenses that outweighed water sales volume of 49 million cbm (+9% YoY). BWE’s 2022 revenue was VND3.5tn (USD147mn; +12% YoY) and its reported NPAT was VND748bn (USD32mn; unchanged YoY), which completed 100% and 102% of our respective full-year forecasts. In addition, BWE’s core NPAT rose 14% YoY and completed 99% of our forecast thanks to the better-than-expected NPAT result of the water segment offsetting the lower-than-expected NPAT result of the waste treatment segment. We see insignificant changes for our 2023F NPAT forecast, pending a fuller review. The water segment’s 2022 NPAT beat our forecast despite slightly lower-than-expected sales volume. Q4 2022 water sales volume rose 9% YoY compared to 9M 2022 growth of 6% YoY. 2022 sales volume growth was 6% YoY and completed 98% of our full-year forecast. However, the water’s segment NPAT-MI was VND582 (USD25mn; +10% YoY) and fulfilled 106% of our full-year forecast. We attribute this outperformance to lower-than-expected depreciation and interest expenses. The waste treatment segment’s 2022 NPAT trailed our forecast. Although this segment’s revenue surpassed our forecast by 10%, its gross profit and NPAT completed only 81% and 20% of our respective full-year forecasts. We will contact BWE for more detail regarding this matter. NPAT of the trading segment in 2022 beat our full-year forecast. BWE achieved huge profits from water pipeline & materials trading in 9M 2022 thanks to high steel prices and low costs. In addition, profit in Q4 2022 remained strong. Figure 1: BWE’s 2022 results Source: BWE, VCSC. (*) Excluding provision/reversal provision expenses for financial investments, unrealized FX loss and other expenses related to COVID-19.

Valuation Target price derivation We continue to apply three valuation methods — DCF, P/E and EV/EBITDA — and derive a blended valuation for POW using weights of 50%, 25% and 25%, respectively. The discounted cash flow method reflects the intrinsic value of the company and is the best method to value the stable cash flow of utilities companies. EV/EBITDA: This method shows the value of a company independent of its gearing structure. Reported P/E: We use reported EPS to value POW; however, we note that as power plants have debt denominated in foreign currency, they suffer forex translation loss/accounting loss from revaluing the remaining loan balance with the current exchange rate every year when the VND depreciates vs foreign currencies (usually USD). Therefore, there is a difference between reported EPS (including forex translation loss) and adjusted EPS (excluding forex translation loss). Realized forex loss, for the principal payment that the power plant pays in a year, is 100% passed through to buyers. We use peer median P/E and EV/EBITDA over the past four years to set target multiples. We apply a 15% discount to our blended valuation to set our target price to reflect our view that the sector currently offers somewhat limited transparency of information for investors. As Vietnam is liberalizing its power sector, there is scope for improving the available information about the pricing mechanism/operation mechanism of the competitive generation/wholesale/retail market, in our view. We continue to apply another 15% discount to our blended valuation due to the uncertainties related to the pending PPA review at the Ca Mau power plants; we will remove this valuation discount once the review is finalized. We believe valuation by the DCF and EV/EBITDA methods is the most appropriate in valuing a power business. Our average fair value for POW generated by these two methods is ~30% and 60% higher than the valuation by P/E method, respectively. We believe P/E based valuations penalize POW as the power business is capital intensive, resulting in significant depreciation and interest expense — in addition to forex translation losses also weighing on EPS. However, we still apply the P/E valuation as this method is simple and transparent. We revise down target price by 2% to VND12,000/share, which is driven by: Lowering our DCF valuation by 3% as we update our DCF models with Q2 2020’s net debt balance, which was ~25% higher vs our last report. A 1% increase in our EV/EBITDA valuation as we revise down our average 2020-2021 EBITDA projection by 1% while raising our target EV/EBITDA multiple from 9.9x to 10.1x. Pleas see the updated multiple on page 10. A 1% cut in our PER valuation as we revise down our average reported 2020-2021 EPS projection by 0.5% and lower our target PER multiple from 14.2x to 14.1x. Please see the updated multiple on page 10. Industrial salt can also be combined with limestone (CaCO3) in a special process to produce soda ash (Na2CO3), which is mostly used for manufacturing construction glass, automobile glass and packaging glass. Caustic soda (NaOH) demand is generally driven by the manufacturing sector. This alkali (hence the name “chlor-alkali”) is a staple chemical of industrial production with diverse applications in numerous industries such as alumina production (largest global consumption share of 18% in 2019, per IHS Markit), pulp & paper, textiles, detergents and cleaning agents. Caustic soda is also used in the treatment of input/drinking water as well as wastewater. Chlorine’s major applications include PVC plastic, bleaching, cleaning and disinfecting. Per IHS Markit, PVC plastics are responsible for one-third of global chlorine consumption. In addition, chlorine can be processed into chlorine compounds with high cleaning, bleaching and disinfecting properties that are applicable in the paper, textile, water treatment and disinfectant industries. Chlorine and caustic soda are co-products — should demand for one product be limited, the other’s output will also be constrained. In developed economies, caustic soda is usually in lower demand than chlorine and thus it is mostly exported. On the other hand, Vietnam has a surplus of chlorine. This contrasting situation is due to Vietnam’s underdeveloped petrochemical industry that limits its production of PVC plastic. Figure 15: Global chlor-alkali value chain and three phases of DGC’s Nghi Son project Source: DGC, IHS Markit, VCSC compilation (joint lines indicate co-requisites or co-products while separate lines indicate different input options and product options) 2. Vietnam’s chlor-alkali industry is underdeveloped Vietnam’s caustic soda imports have been rising quickly as domestic producers face high barriers to entry. From 2009-2019, Vietnam’s imports of caustic soda (mostly from China and Taiwan) recorded a value CAGR of 11% and a volume CAGR of 15% to reach more than 282,000 tons in 2019, per ITC. Meanwhile, total domestic capacity amounted to only 185,000 tons — approximately 40% of total domestic demand — and it is concentrated within a few producers that have almost reached full capacity utilization. Despite the State’s ambitious plans and instructions to expand the domestic basic chemical industry, chemical production has long been frowned upon by local authorities due to environmental risks. In addition, industrial salt — the key input to chlor-alkali — is subject to an import quota that is individually assigned for each importer while domestic salt is not of sufficient quality. As such, not only do chlor-alkali producers need technical capabilities as well as capital for setting up production facilities and environmental measures, but lobbying power is also required to secure an investment license and import quotas. Exemplifying this situation is the fact that most incumbent players are SOEs, privatized SOEs and private companies whose owners have work experience at SOEs. Incumbent players consist of either SOEs with limited capacity headroom or private producers with small commercial output (Figure 16). In southern Vietnam, Vedan Vietnam Ltd. (a Taiwanese food seasoning producer) and South Basic Chemical JSC (HOSE: CSV) are the only manufacturers of chlor-alkali chemicals. While Vedan has a larger production capacity between the two, most of its output is used in-house. Meanwhile, CSV — a subsidiary of Vinachem — has been at full capacity for a long time. However, CSV’s capacity expansion project has stalled for several years — similar to other chemical subsidiaries of Vinachem — due to Vinachem’s demand for high cash dividends that has constrained resources for investments as well as the complex investment approval process that SOEs typically have to undergo. A similar situation applies to Viet Tri Chemical

Context:
capacity to expand 4x over the next 10 years, spurring growth We forecast Brent oil prices to remain high in 2023 at USD90/bbl (6% lower than consensus) and USD75/bbl in 2024-2026F (~12% lower than consensus). In 2022, the average Brent oil prices rose 39.2% YoY to ~USD99/bbl due to 1) the Russia-Ukraine conflict tightening the global oil supply, 2) OPEC+ switching from a production increase to a production cut, and 3) a recovery of oil demand. ​The International Energy Agency (IEA), Energy Information Association (EIA) and OPEC expect global oil demand to rise by 1.7 million barrels per day (bpd) in 2023 to 102 million bpd, which would surpass the pre-COVID level. Key risks to our oil price forecasts include 1) a global economic recession that would weigh on oil demand and 2) a less aggressive view from OPEC+ that would lead to a lower future production cut than the current level. Gas prices to be higher in 2023. The IEA expects high gas prices will last into 2023 — with the average gas/LNG price in Japan, US and EU at ~USD36/MMBTU (+23% YoY) — due to Russia’s uncertain gas supply. In addition, Chicago Mercantile Exchange (CME) futures as of October 2022 imply average LNG prices of USD31/MMBTU in 2023. We expect oil prices of USD75-90/bbl in 2023-2026F and the recently approved oil & gas law to support the development of new domestic gas projects (total estimated capex of at least USD11bn) such as Block B and Blue Whale, which would benefit GAS, PVS and PVD. Strong growth of gas demand. Per the September 2022 draft of Vietnam’s national energy development plan, the country’s gas & LNG-fired power plant capacity will expand 4x over the next 10 years. As such, Vietnam will be thirsty for gas starting in 2023. We estimate a gas deficit of 1.2 billion cubic meters (bcm) in 2023 that will increase to ~7 bcm by 2026, making LNG import projects critical. We expect the monopoly gas supplier in Vietnam, GAS (our top pick), will benefit from its first LNG project (Thi Vai) beginning in 2023 with a ~7% net profit contribution. GAS aims to triple the Thi Vai LNG terminal’s capacity to 3-6 million tonnes p.a. (MMTPA) and develop the Son My LNG terminal (3-6 MMTPA) in 2024. PVS is also a beneficiary of the capacity expansion of LNG-fired plants in Vietnam. We estimate total capital expenditure (capex) of at least USD24bn for LNG infrastructure installation. Growth in petroleum consumption to benefit BSR, PVT & PLX. The September 2022 draft of the national energy development plan states that Vietnam’s petroleum consumption will rise 4.1% p.a. in 2022-2026F, which nearly quadruples the IEA’s expected global growth rate. We believe BSR (our second top pick) has fallen below its intrinsic value. We forecast BSR’s gross refining margin (GRM) at USD8.6/bbl in 2023 vs its record high GRM of USD12.5/bbl in 2022. In 2023, we expect BSR’s NPAT will drop 50% YoY to VND7.4tn, but this is close to the previous cyclical peak in 2017. BSR’s valuation is attractive at a 2023F P/B of 0.8x. Moreover, BSR’s share price plunged ~50% in H2 2022, underperforming Brent oil prices and regional peers (their share price move range from -28% to +8%) due to a sharp correction in the Vietnamese equity market.​ Bright outlook for crude & oil product tanker rates in 2023. According to Clarksons and oil transporter PVT, the crude tanker rate outlook remains positive as rates doubled in 2022. PVT should benefit from a recovery of transportation demand and higher freight rates. We like PLX’s recovery outlook due

Expecting steady recovery from Q3 2021 low We raise our target price (TP) by 0.8% to VND37,700/share and maintain our OUTPERFORM rating for DRC. Our higher TP is mainly driven by (1) the effect of rolling our target price horizon to end-2022F and (2) our higher aggregate 2021-2028F NPAT-MI forecast (+4.9% vs our previous forecast), which are partially offset by our higher WACC (12.2% vs 11.6% in our previous forecast) resulting from our higher beta assumption for DRC and changes in our in-house cost of equity assumptions. We cut our 2021F NPAT-MI 15.3% to VND303bn (USD13.2mn; +18.2% YoY), which is due to (1) our lower gross margin assumptions for all of DRC’s product segments to reflect lower-than-expected margins in Q3 2021 and (2) a 31.7% increase in our SG&A expense projection. For 2022F, we forecast revenue of VND4.9tn (USD214.4mn; +14.2% YoY) and NPAT-MI of VND419bn (USD18.2mn; +38.3% YoY). We expect the top line to be mainly driven by strong demand for DRC’s radial tires; meanwhile, the bottom line will be bolstered by (1) improved radial tire GPM and (2) a reduction in SG&A expenses to a normal level from a high base in 2021F. We raise our aggregate 2023-2028F NPAT-MI forecast by 6.5% mainly thanks to the accelerated development of DRC’s radial tire factory phase 3, which we expect to add 200,000 units p.a. to the current capacity of 600,000 units p.a. starting from H2 2022F and another 200,000 units p.a. from 2024F. As a result, we expect DRC’s radial factory to reach 82%/90%/95%/98%/100% of its 1,000,000-unit p.a. capacity in 2023/24/25/26/27F, respectively. Downside risks to our positive view: Higher-than-expected increase in natural rubber prices; intensified competition in bias tire market. Sales volume to recover from Q4 2021F. Although Q3 2021 was a lackluster quarter due to COVID-19, 9M 2021 bias tire sales volume remained resilient as it increased 14.5% YoY. We attribute this optimistic result to the fact that DRC is facing less competition from large Chinese tire manufacturers that focus on radial tires and mass bias tire products rather than customized bias tires — the niche that DRC is capitalizing on. We expect strong demand for DRC’s radial tire segment will continue in Q4 2021F and 2022F because DRC’s radial tires are not impacted by antidumping and countervailing taxes in its major export markets (i.e., Brazil and the US). As such, we forecast DRC’s 2021F bias tire and radial tire sales volumes to increase 5.8% and 20.1% YoY, respectively. Radial tire GPM to trend down from 2023F as depreciation costs start to kick in from H2 2022F. We trim our 2021F radial tire GPM assumption to 16.7% vs 17.2% in our previous forecast to reflect the low margin in Q3 2021. In addition, we expect radial tire GPM to peak at 17.3% in 2022F thanks to strong demand from export markets, which more than offsets the impact of depreciation costs for phase 3 of the radial tire factory. From 2023F onward, we model the depreciation costs to fully kick in, which slightly drags radial tire GPM. 9M 2021 Recap: Lackluster Q3 2021 after impressive H1 2021 results Figure 1: 9M 2021 results Source: Company’s financial statements, VCSC 2021F Outlook: Resilient demand to drive Q4 recovery Figure 2: 2021 forecasts 2022F Outlook: Radial tire segment to drive strong net profit growth Figure 3: 2022 forecasts Source: Company’s financial statements, VCSC forecast Valuation We value DRC using a DCF valuation method and arrive at a target price of VND37,700/share, implying a projected total stock return of 15.0%, inclusive of a 4.4% dividend yield. We maintain our explicit forecast period to 2028

Upbeat five-year profit plan for 2022-2026 We attended IDC’s annual general meeting (AGM) in HCMC on April 19. Management guides for IDC’s 2022 total income (including revenue, financial income and other gains) of VND8tn (USD347mn; +61% YoY) and PBT of VND2.8tn (USD120mn; +267% YoY), representing 110% and 107% of our full-year forecasts, respectively. Management also shared that IDC’s Q1 2022 preliminary earnings could achieve 25% of its full-year guidance, which is equivalent to Q1 2022 preliminary PBT of ~VND690bn (USD30mn; +6.4x YoY). Shareholders approved a FY2021 dividend of 10% in stock and VND3,000/share (5% dividend yield — which is in line with our current expectation). IDC already paid the interim cash dividend of VND2,400/share for FY2021; the remaining VND600/share is expected to be paid in 2022. Shareholders approved a FY2022 dividend of 40% on par value. The proportion between cash and stock will be determined later. We currently assume a FY2022 cash dividend of VND4,000/share — equivalent to a dividend yield of 6.6%. Shareholders approved to cancel IDC’s rights issuance plan that was approved at the April 2021 AGM as the company is currently able to arrange its capital for its future expansion plan. Shareholders approved the resignations of the chairman of the board of directors (BOD) Mr. Le Ba Tho and Board of Supervisors (BOS) member Ms. Tran Thanh Linh. In addition, shareholders appointed Ms. Nguyen Thi Nhu Mai as a BOD member and Mr. Vo Tan Dung as a BOS member. Upbeat five-year plan for 2022-2026. Management released its business plan for 2022-2026 (see details in Figure 1). In addition to strong profit growth guidance for 2022, IDC guides for 2022-2026 CAGRs for revenue and NPAT-MI of 23% and 20%, respectively. Management guidance is more confident than our forecasts, and we believe the gap is mainly due to higher-than-expected profit recognition for IP land sales over the period. Figure 1: IDC’s five-year business plan Source: IDC, VCSC forecasts BOD changes are to prepare for IDC’s next growth phases. Management shared that after being a fully private owned company, IDC plans to restructure the company and its subsidiaries to improve operational efficiency. Mr. Le Ba Tho is highly experienced in corporate restructuring and had joined IDC’s BOD to contribute his expertise to company’s restructuring plan; however, he asked to resign as he is occupied with his personal work schedule. Ms. Nguyen Thi Nhu Mai was appointed as BOD member. She is CEO of SSG Group, which is highly experienced in the real estate industry. Management shared that IDC plans to expand its IP projects and residential real estate projects near its future IPs. Management hopes Ms. Nguyen Thi Nhu Mai will contribute to IDC’s expansion plans due to her real estate expertise. Management shared its expansion plan for the IP segment, reaffirming our forecasts. Management shared that IDC will expand ~1,500 - 2,000 ha of IP land area over the next five years, which is more ambitious than our current expectation that IDC will expand ~2,000 ha of IP land in northern and southern industrial areas in 2022-2030. Management shared that although several provinces have invited IDC to invest in IP projects, the company targets a sustainable expansion plan that prioritizes feasible projects in key industrial provinces.

Context:
Giá cổ phiếu điều chỉnh về mức hấp dẫn Chúng tôi nâng khuyến nghị dành cho CTCP Bán lẻ Kỹ thuật số FPT (FRT) từ KHẢ QUAN lên MUA sau khi giá cổ phiếu đã điều chỉnh 9% trong tháng qua. Định giá tỏ ra hấp dẫn với PEG 3 năm là 0,7, dựa trên các dự báo không đổi. Chúng tôi cho rằng nguyên nhân khiến giá cổ phiếu diễn biến kém so với thị trường chung là nhà đầu tư lo ngại tình hình biên lợi nhuận gộp từ iPhone sau KQLN Quý 4/2018 kém. Tuy nhiên, chúng tôi cho rằng thị trường đã phản ứng thái quá vì chúng tôi kỳ vọng biên lợi nhuận gộp từ iPhone sẽ cải thiện trong 6 tháng cuối năm. Tuy nhiên, chúng tôi điều chỉnh giảm giá mục tiêu 8% sau khi điều chỉnh gỉam PER mục tiêu cho mảng điện thoại từ 13 lần xuống 12 lần (thấp hơn 20% so với PER mục tiêu của chúng tôi đưa ra cho các mảng kinh doanh điện thoại và điện tử tiêu dùng của MWG). Chúng tôi cho rằng mức này là phù hợp hơn với FRT do quy mô nhỏ hơn và tiềm năng tăng trưởng kém vững chắc hơn so với MWG vì MWG đang tăng trưởng mạnh mẽ trong mảng điện tử tiêu dùng. Chúng tôi cho rằng việc nhập trực tiếp phụ kiện sẽ hỗ trợ lợi nhuận FRT năm 2019. Đối với các năm 2020 và 2021, ngoài việc biên lợi nhuận gộp iPhone phục hồi, chúng tôi dự báo lợi nhuận sẽ được củng cố nhờ các chương trình F.Friends và trợ giá nhà mạng cũng như mở rộng chuỗi nhà thuốc. Rủi ro đối với quan điểm lạc quan của chúng tôi: Biên lợi nhuận gộp từ kinh doanh iPhone phục hồi chậm hơn so với dự kiến. Biên lợi nhuận gộp từ iPhone dự kiến sẽ tiếp tục thấp trong 6 tháng đầu năm 2019 do lượng hàng tồn kho cũ. Các doanh nghiệp bán lẻ, trong đó có MWG và FRT, đã ước tính nhu cầu iPhone mới cao hơn so với thực tế, khiến lượng iPhone tồn kho tính đến cuối năm 2018 ở mức cao. Như đã đề cập trong báo cáo cập nhật Doanh số iPhone mới yếu ảnh hưởng đến biên lợi nhuận ngày 14/02/2019, chúng tôi nhận thấy các doanh nghiệp bán lẻ tích cực giảm giá đối với iPhone trong Quý 1/2019. Vì vậy, chúng tôi dự báo biên lợi nhuận gộp từ iPhone sẽ tiếp tục yếu trong 6 tháng đầu năm 2019. Chúng tôi kỳ vọng việc đẩy mạnh mảng phụ kiện, có biên lợi nhuận cao, sẽ hỗ trợ lợi nhuận 2019. FRT đã bắt đầu nhập phụ kiện trực tiếp từ các nhà sản xuất Trung Quốc từ tháng 01/2019, qua đó đa dạng hóa các mặt hàng phụ kiện kinh doanh và giảm giá bán khoảng 20% trong khi vẫn tăng được biên lợi nhuận. Công ty đề ra mục tiêu doanh thu từ phụ kiện năm 2019 sẽ tăng mạnh 65% và biên lợi nhuận gộp mảng này sẽ tăng mạnh 10 điểm % lên 50%. Vì công ty có kế hoạch sẽ tung ra nhiều chương trình khuyến mãi và đào tạo nhân viên để thúc đẩy hoạt động kinh doanh, chúng tôi dự báo lợi nhuận gộp từ phụ kiện sẽ tăng mạnh 63% trong năm 2019 và chiếm khoảng 20% tổng lợi nhuận gộp so với khoáng 14% năm 2018. Context:
100% gas thermal power plants, 70% urea production, and 100% industrial parks in the country. GAS has a 70% market share in the LPG wholesale business and supplies liquefied natural gas (LNG). In our November 2024 Update Report, we lowered our target price (TP) for GAS by 5.2% to VND77,300/share due to a 5.7% reduction in our aggregate 2025-2029F NPAT-MI forecast (respective changes of -6.8%/-4.4%/-7.0%/-7.4%/-3.5% for 2025/26/27/28/29F) but maintain our OUTPERFORM rating. Our forecast cuts are driven by our (1) 7.9%/4.6% lower projections for the average Brent/FO prices, as published in our November 14 Oil & Gas Sector Update, and (2) a 14% lower LNG tariff (including storage, regasification, distribution fees) from ~USD3.0/MMBTU to USD2.6/MMBTU on average for 2025-2029, as per further clarification with power plants. These together outweigh (3) our 1% higher aggregate gas sales volume assumptions (mainly from higher LNG volume), and (4) 11% higher aggregate LPG sales volume and higher GPM following 9M results. We project flat 2025F NPAT-MI YoY due to (1) higher gas sales volumes, primarily LNG, (2) a reduced loss from Thi Vai LNG (VND118bn vs VND628bn in 2024), and (3) no provision expense as in 2024, offsetting the negative impact of a forecast 12.8% YoY decline in FO prices. We expect LNG Thi Vai’s volume to surge in the coming years, partly supported by our projection of an LNG price peak in 2025/26F. We anticipate LNG prices to ease from 2027F, boosting sales to power plants and industrial customers in the medium to long term. We forecast an EPS CAGR of 16% for 2024-2028F. We view GAS’s projected 2025F P/E of 14.7x as undemanding vs the five-year average of our selected regional peers of 16.0x. GAS’s net cash/equity is also higher than the peer average. Upside potential: Higher-than-expected Brent, FO prices; LNG pricing mechanism approval; earlier reversal of a VND815bn bad debt provision in 2025F vs our projection in 2026F. Downside risk: Lower-than-expected volume to power plants. Vietnam’s Petroleum Sector Vietnam’s petroleum consumption to grow at a 4.1% CAGR in 2023-29F 28 Vietnam's petroleum consumption set for continued growth with sufficient supply 28 Vietnam’s petroleum consumption and PLX’s domestic volume growth (%) Vietnam’s petroleum consumption forecasts (million tonnes) Source: Vietcap estimates, MoIT, IEA, BSR, PLX. Note: (*) Vietcap’s estimates based on production, and import and export numbers from the MoIT and General Statistics Office of Vietnam (GSO). 29 Source: MoIT, MoF, PLX, OIL, Vietcap Further revisions to petroleum regulations to boost earnings 29 Comparison between drafts 3 & 4 of the new decree vs the current decree 30 Source: Vietcap estimates More proposals for the new Decree According to local media, the Ministry of Industry and Trade (MoIT) has finalized the 4th Draft Decree on petroleum businesses, incorporating feedback from the Vietnam Petroleum Association (VINPA) and industry players, and plans to submit the draft to the Government for approval. Key details are below: 1. Pricing mechanisms: The MoIT has proposed two options for Government consideration: Maintain Government oversight while giving petrol distributors more pricing flexibility. The regulated operating costs would be adjusted annually based on Vietnam’s CPI (Draft 4 as stated in our Petroleum Sector Report). Allow full market-based pricing, giving petrol distributors full control over wholesale and retail prices, as well as timing. The Government should provide the price formula, and the international reference price and premium, with no ceiling prices or regulated operating cost and profit. 2. Reducing intermediaries: The MoIT has proposed two options for Government consideration: Wholesale distributors can only purchase from major distributors, not from each other (Draft 4, as stated in our Petroleum Sector Report).
